But actually, Dimitri has done an amazing job writing an explainer that goes through all these concepts in quite some depth, but in a very readable way. Dimitri Lascarisoni-And I promise, there is no Comic Sans on that page. Just if you're scared. You can also read the CSS variables spec and understand more how that stuff works, all the amazing stuff you can do with it. It's really more powerful than it looks.

SPEAKER_1: And the other thing is that there is actually a lot of really cool discussions happening right now in your present about the web components and working out all the corner cases and working out the kinks of how things should work precisely. I mean, we're in the future. All those things are solved. And we don't have to worry about them. But you guys have to worry about them. Because getting this stuff done just right and making sure this does not suck. Is really, really important. So please, if you have any gumption for bike shedding or really, really endless discussions with no particular meaning, please join the standards list and start listening in and start understanding what's going on and what's happening.

But it's also possible to start building with this stuff today. So we, Dimitri's written a polyfill for web components. All it requires is Shadow DOM, so you would have to turn that on in Chrome to play around with this. That's a really cool thing that actually makes a polyfill for this entire thing. Everything we showed you today. That's how we're actually making this run.

SPEAKER_1: JAREK WILKIEWICZ-You know those self-driving cars? It's like that. It's like a very, very early prototype that you can possibly drive. Bad things may happen, and I'm not, you know, I can't guarantee.

JAREK WILKIEWICZ-Don't use that one for anything in production today. But actually, there's a really cool library written by Daniel Buchner, who I believe is right there from Mozilla. He's built this really cool library called XTags. XTags is basically a polyfill for the custom elements part of what we showed you today. And the cool thing about that is this actually works in all major browsers. It uses a number of very clever hacks to do it. But you actually can start building real apps on top of this today if you want with custom elements.

SPEAKER_1: And moreover, Daniel is actually active at work building a pretty, pretty solid toolkit on top of this, the UI widget library that would potentially power some really, really cool applications in the future.

JAREK WILKIEWICZ-So it's really cool. So check that out once we post the link to the post page. All right. JAREK WILKIEWICZ-Great. So that's it. That's all we got for you today. We wanted to share a little bit more about Web Components, this really exciting thing from the future. Thanks so much for coming out.
SPEAKER_1: JAREK WILKIEWICZ-The future boss leaves in 10 minutes. But until then, we can answer your questions if you'd like.
JAREK WILKIEWICZ-Just come up to one of these microphones that we've got here in the aisles. What about events was the question that we just got from the audience?

SPEAKER_1: The events are actually also handled by the Shadow DOM specifications, and there's really neat things that we do to make sure that the information in the event never escapes outside of the Shadow DOM subtree, but the events are heard. It's called retargeting. And what happens is that if something fires, like, for example, if you click a play button in your video element, the click event propagates out, but it looks like it came from the video element. Does that make sense? So it's actually a really complex part of these things. And we thought extremely hard to make sure that Shadow Dump feels transparent to the user. And it doesn't look like it's affecting any actual normal operation of the events. But the information from the inside of the Shadow Dump subtree never leaks out on the outside.
Cool. So we've got a question. This is Mike.

SPEAKER_2: So it sounds like a lot of this is helping you with bringing in dependencies and encapsulating code. Have you guys thought much in the spec about dependency management? Obviously, a lot of Java developers and a lot of developers in general use Maven for that, so you don't get dependency explosion inside your app. There's that encapsulated part, but in a team, you want to try and use consistent dependencies across. Has that been addressed in this?

SPEAKER_1: At this point, there is just early discussions on how this actual linking of the custom elements, the packaging, will happen. So this is actually a good time to start talking and start discussing this thing. I mean, we solved it in the future, and you saw it works. But later on.

SPEAKER_4: What about with media queries, where the widget The component needs to adapt based on certain breakpoints. And usually those breakpoints are going to be different based on whatever context of the app they're used in.

SPEAKER_1: So one of the interesting things there is that, first of all, we made sure that Shadow DOM actually operates within the confines of CSS. We're not inventing anything normal. So all of the CSS machinery, the way you operate on it, will just work. It will feel like it's just part of the document. So it will get all of the same information about the size and media queries and things like that.
And if you have a follow-up question, we can answer that also afterwards as well.

SPEAKER_6: I'm an extension developer in user script aficionado. And there are lots of templated sites out there that don't use this yet. But that would be great to extend. And are you thinking of how to integrate this into the Chrome extension API so that we can add components where there are none and change them?

SPEAKER_1: Shadow DOM is actually one of the really, really useful cases for extensions, the ones that modify the page on the fly. Because what you can do is you can build a Shadow DOM into an element, and the document doesn't know that it's there. And so, yes, it's a very, very common use case. Thank you. It's coming. Soon.

SPEAKER_3: I'm a Google Maps developer, but I was thinking, could you embed the Map API into the Shadow DOM so you could build a custom tag, for example? Store locator and give that to a customer and say, hey, do this, and put a page into that. David Chandler-Yes, you could actually.
That's a phenomenal use case, is doing exactly that. In fact, one of the components in X-Tags today is a graph map. You should talk to this guy.
SPEAKER_1: He's already got one.
David Chandler-Yeah. So that's an awesome use case for it.

SPEAKER_1: Come on. David Chandler-Anything else? Bring it on.

SPEAKER_5: What about SEO? There's potential information, like the side tag, that would be less important. Would Googlebot pick that up because it's not potentially rendered on the page?
So we don't work on the search team, so we don't know the answer to that question. But we can imagine there's going to be some interesting changes in the way that people construct their sites.
SPEAKER_1: It doesn't have to be on a side tag. It can be an H1 or bold if you wanted to. It's up to you to define it as the author of the API.
Cool. And if there are any other questions, Dimitri and I will be hanging around for a few minutes afterwards if you want to ask us any other questions. Thanks so much. Yay. Thank you.

source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/transcripts/transcript-Google I_O 2012 - The Web Platform's Cutting Edge.m4a_spaced.txt

messages.content: SPEAKER_0: Welcome, welcome, welcome. It's good to be here. I'm going to do a little bit of an overview of who we are in case you're just wandering by and you're curious what is happening at this booth. We are Merck, KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany. In the U. S., we are known as Millipore Sigma, EMD Sirono, and EMD Electronics. EMD Electronics is the one who's hosting this booth here. We are a 300-and 57-year-old company in Germany. We're the oldest pharmaceutical company in the world, but we do a lot more than pharma now. So, we have any of the reagents that you see in any lab to do any kind of scientific experiment. We are at the core of all of the technology that you see here. Historically, a lot in the display layer, and now very much in the chipset underlying it. Our goal is sparking discovery and elevating humanity. We are a company of nerdy scientists who love to create amazing things. For me, one of the things I think is not discussed as much about our history, but is really important and important to what we do on the EMD digital side is in May of 1851, Emmanuel Marc put the first guarantee on a bottle of reagents. And it sounds very, very simple, right? Very basic that I personally guarantee the purity of my preparation. But it's critical for science and for scientific reproducibility. Prior to this, I had no idea if the thing that you were using was actually this thing. And I view this sense of quality as being something that is more and more existentially important. And I'll have my good friend Alex coming up and joining me in just a second here. We've talked a lot about this over the last few years. We've had a number of interesting technology waves and just distribution of proliferation of technology. If we go back to 1980s to today, the average bandwidth per household is increased by about 50% a year, every year. It's 10% less than Moore's law. We had the last 10 years'92% reduction in the cost to transmit a megabit of information. Information distribution has just gotten unbelievably fast and unbelievably cheap. And in 2010, we came up with something incredible. We monetized eyeballs with online advertising, and it led to this huge amount of information being created, consumed, and distributed globally. And it led to a lot of kind of scary information that was coming up, but we were incentivizing it. Throughout that entire time, we had Moore's Law just continue to tick along. Compute continued to become cheaper and more powerful. And when we combine this massive amount of information that's available with this compute that you can do something with, you wind up with the LLMs that we're seeing today, the generative models that you see today. And this means that we're now at this point where information creation is now unbelievably easy to create and distribute. And the need for an ability to pull back and see where did information come from becomes accessibly important. And that's exactly what we do on the EMD digital side. It's can we create this bridge of quality for information and tracing it back? Can we take a data point and trace it back to source system and validate the source system itself? So we've got two fun portions here. The first one is I'd like to introduce my good friend Alex. We're going to have a chat for about 20 minutes or so, and then we'll have a fun discussion on mTrust. Yes, A few words. Alex is kind of a Google legend. He created one of the great guides for new Googlers as they were onboarding as a product manager there. He was the head of strategy at Stripe for a few years and just co-founded Common Tools. And I'll let him talk a little bit about that when he comes up. But Alex, please come on up. And I mean, we met.three years ago at a Google conference in Paris of all the APMs and had a long discussion specifically on the sense of information creation or information distribution, infinitely fast, infinitely free, information creation, infinitely fast, infinitely free, and this almost like existential sense of, you know, in existentialism, there's this sense of meaning and authenticity as like the critical human factor. And so as we look at like the human and the AI side, the human is such a critical part of this that I think often doesn't get discussed as much.

Yeah, I think it's one of those things. And by the way, this is missing a couple of words. Former head of corporate strategy at Stripe, not head of Stripe. That's correct. Just for the record. I think when you look at, I think from cost comes meaning to some degree. The fact that you choose to invest the cost in the thing gives that thing that you produce a fair bit of meaning. And as the cost of that production continues to decline, and it becomes incredibly easy to make AI-generated slop, you get a cacophony of background noise, of just kind of middling stuff. But I think there's a depressive view of this, which is just that we're going to be inundated with never-ending AI-generated slop, which I think is probably to some degree is going to happen. But I think there's also a possibility in this world to have a new style of co-creation with AI. And so one of my friends I was talking to a while ago is like some guy in tech. And he was telling me about how he uses, he's like, yeah, I started writing poetry again. I did it in college. I haven't done it for decades. And I started writing poetry because I could work with Claude. And I could just talk back and forth about a theme I wanted to capture or try to tweak it. And I think that's a great positive example of the kind of value that comes when you have a co-creative partner as an LLM. That allows you to reduce the cost of exploration and discovery as an individual.

SPEAKER_0: Oh, I completely agree. I think we have, like, a renaissance that's coming on just, like, creative arts in general, right? Because this idea that there's, like, this judgment-free creative partner to be able to work with and do it. I mean, we were talking earlier, I spend a lot of time making songs with this on different topics. Currently working on Mr. Magoo's parents, how they met. Kind of curious. But where else would you have the time to be able to put something together like that and do it? It is genuinely extraordinary to see it and how the improvement has come and how it's changed is just wild to me. Now with that, you also have a couple of curses from like a technology standpoint, right? Like there's this curse of recursion where I still find it fascinating that the top models in the world are only trained on information up to the end of 2022. So, you're like most recent updates to GPT and Gemini, the data for the foundation model ends at the end of 2022, because from that point forward, there is no sense of whether the content was generated by a human or not. And if you train a model on outputs from models, it leads to model collapse. Everything reverts to mean.

The models, to some degree, I think it was like they pull towards the centroid. They pull towards the center. They give you the most average answer to that thing. It's like Family Feud on steroids. And so you get that in inference. You get that in training. And if you give more of that kind of middling slop into the process that you're distilling the patterns out of, you get closer and closer to this boring HR corp speak kind of society. So yeah, you've got to figure out how to get the interesting novel things out of these and not have them just give you the most bland possible answer to whatever question you give them.

SPEAKER_0: And being able to tie back and get interesting things, I mean, these are human things. These are distinctly human things. And knowing that it's generated by a human, I think it's going to be an incredibly challenging problem to solve overall. Do you mind talking just a little bit about Common Tools and what you're working on now?

Sure. So we're a seed stage startup. We're based in Berkeley. And what we're trying to do is unbundle the app for the AI era. So we think that if you apply AI to the way that software works today, you get kind of boring, hyper-centralized apps. We think that AI instead could help unleash a whole new class of software that is totally personal, perfectly personal, where your data you hold on your turf, and the software just kind of automatically emerges out of your data, helping you do whatever you're trying to do with that. And I think this is one of those things that sounds crazy because we're so used to in the entire industry that software is expensive to write and cheap to run. These are two fundamental assumptions that have been true for the entire software industry forever. Turns out LLMs undermine both of those because software that uses LLMs to execute is now has a marginal cost, is now no longer free effectively. And secondarily, it's now LLMs have made it possible to generate crappy little simple bits of software for free, which radically changes the kinds of cost structures and what kinds of centralization forces make sense. And so we think that AI can be this unlock for not hyper-aggregation into one-size-fits-none apps, and instead into this perfectly personal world of co-creativity and control of your own data.

SPEAKER_0: It's really wild because you think, too, the greatest thing in my mind would be I'm a news junkie, reading through news constantly, and to be able to reference back that direct lineage and sequence of things that I saw. I find that one of the most interesting ways to augment one's experience and one's mind overall would be one. A lot of this requires almost this local processing.

Yeah, you have to have it if your data is somewhere else that you don't really have control. So if you use a lot of Google services today, and I worked at Google for 13 years. I think it's an amazing company. But as you put more and more of your data into these services, they have a fair bit of it on you. But they also, it's hard for them to add features. So, like, you know, say I was into tabletop role-playing games. I'm not, but I have a lot of friends who are. And let's imagine there's some amazing feature if they tied together data from Gmail and Calendar and Search into a thing that would change my life as a tabletop role-playing enthusiast. But there's no way Google would ever build that feature. Can you imagine as a product manager inside of the company, come up with this idea, and then you go talk to 12 other product managers throughout the company and convince them to implement this feature? And each one of them says, how many more daily active users will I get? You go, I don't know, 50,000 maybe? And they go, I can't even count that low. So you get this as more data centralizes, you get actually less value out of it. You get more of the lowest common denominator of one size fits none kind of software. And that's where we are in the technology industry. And I think that AI LLMs are a truly disruptive technology. I think they change the cost of a number of inputs. It has really interesting implications for what kinds of things can now be possible that we just didn't even know to ask for because we didn't know it was possible.

SPEAKER_0: It also brings up, what is the value of the output from this? Because if you suddenly dilute everything with content that's coming out or generated information, How do you then start valuing each incremental piece that's there? And if it is consumed only by one versus something that can be consumed by many, how do you manage that? A lot of it comes down also to the compute side. What's the capacity? Who is going to have this? Where is it going to be?

And a lot of software, one of the reasons that software is hard to write and expensive is generalizing it. You have to make sure it works for lots of different situations, lots of different use cases, lots of different people. If you're going to have a piece of software that you're going to run a single time, it's fine. It can be kind of sloppy. It can be kind of duct-taped together. It's fine. And that changes the structure of the kinds of things that you expect software to do, and it changes the kinds of use cases that are now plausible.
SPEAKER_0: Do you think this changes the folks who are going into computer science right now?

I think LLMs change a lot of things. I think the people who use them most effectively are people who know how to use them and, again, co-create with them. It won't replace your thinking. It will replace it with a more general, sort of muddled, simple. But if you can use it to help lever your creative thinking and let you pull on threads more quickly or explore ideas farther, that's the kind of thing that will unlock quite a bit. So one of my good friends is Simon Willison, and he covers a lot of stuff about AI and has a very popular blog on this. And he constantly will now make little bits of software that would have taken him an afternoon to write. And now they take him 30 seconds. And so now he has all these little pieces of software that it would have never made sense for him to invest that time. This is somebody who's a wizard. He built Django, the web framework, many years ago. And that's an amazingly productive engineer. So, I think seeing how you can change the way that you create and the kinds of things that you can create with it. If you assume this thing, you're going to give it a prompt. And you're going to say, build software that does this. And it'll produce a perfectly.a perfect bit of software that does exactly that, you're going to be disappointed. If you think of it instead as something that's going to help extend and allow you to think with higher leverage and move faster through the boring parts of the problem, I think you'll have a lot more.

SPEAKER_0: Yeah. I mean, we talked earlier. Both Alex and I have two kids each under the age of five, right? And most of the time is spent with handing them phones to talk to GPT and, like, have these conversations back and forth to, like, being able to create kind of whatever they want. And we were talking earlier. I find it fascinating, this idea of taking a child and never giving them a sense of constraint, like, for creation. Like, if you have a concept, like, I want to make this thing, and it's going to be a combination of this, this, and this, and then it suddenly appears, right? And that sense of like, can you do it? Yes, you absolutely can. You can always do this. What that means for almost like a generation of people that will come through.

When Adeline asks, you know, questions, she's super curious, obviously, and she'll ask these questions. And sometimes I'll be, you know, not a finance dad moment, but I'll be like, oh, God, I'll give her a short answer. But then I'll say, Adeline, I'll just ask ChatGPT. And she has a conversation with it for minutes. And she asks all these follow-up questions. And why do penguins not eat meat? And how come they don't fly? And by knowing she's a five-year-old to make it appropriate for her level, she just knows that she can ask this thing that has infinite patience. I think that's one of the things that LLMs are great at is human-style common sense. And sort of background knowledge of the world. They're very well read. They're kooky. They get the answers wrong sometimes. But they have infinite patience. And they never get tired of the question to ask. And that allows, just imagine having a conversation partner who knows so much about the world, who's never going to make you feel like an idiot, and is always going to be game for whatever thread you want to pull on. And I think that just changes the way that you think about learning and curiosity and discovery. And it's really cool to watch Adeline do that.

SPEAKER_0: The thing I get a little nervous about, when it becomes more of like, here's the question, here's the answer, right? Where is that process of trying to do diligence? Where did this answer come from? Is this answer legitimate or not? And that sense of truth is also very complicated because there are very few things in the world that are actually like this or this, and it tends to be a spectrum.
I think for factual questions with a numerical answer, don't use it. For general vibes or riffing on different ideas, I find it really useful.
SPEAKER_0: But do you see it changing? Do you see a path whereby there is a mechanism to be able to create more of this, almost just linkage?

I think there has to be. This is where LLMs are clearly an extremely useful part of their extremely new, useful material. Think of it like magical duct tape. Magical duct tape on its own isn't particularly useful. The question is what you duct tape together. So it's like a thing that allows you to extend the possibility of thinking for a lot of things. That means, though, that there will be I think increased need for trust, for provenance of sources, for knowing the supply chain of your information, and using the LLM not as an oracle that gives you the precise answer to the thing, but as the glue, as the magical duct tape, the human-style kind of common sense that can help you reason through and connect more formal systems of factual databases or sources or what have you.

SPEAKER_0: What's your take on the hardware side? Because I like to, no offense, I do like to joke that mostly when I talk to software folks, they tend to view chips as like breadboards that suddenly got smaller. Is that not how it works? I mean, it's close. But I mean, what's your sense from a hardware side? Because you very much come from their software side of things. A lot of these folks here are very much like chips, hardware people, not breadboards. How do you see the interplay there? What do you view from a constraint standpoint? Because I think oftentimes as you go further into the chain, so much of this is powered by by compute, like the amount of RAM I can have on a GPU, the number of those GPUs I can string together is going to dictate the amount of information I can actually put into my model. And those have a lot of constraints downstream. The other weird thing is at each point upstream of it, you add another zero to the value that's created. And so at the end of this application side, you have the highest value that's created. I was looking at Google, Facebook, and Microsoft; they made double what NVIDIA made last year in profit. Wow. Okay. And it was about $200 billion combined between them versus the 100 on the NVIDIA side. It was up through Q3 of last year. Which also, to put that in context, is like four or five chip sacks in a single year. And so there's a part of me also that wonders, like, is there going to be an invest that comes in more from that angle to be like, I'm computationally constrained in some ways for the first time because, you know, I can only put maybe less than 30 seconds worth of data that Google would generate on any given day into training data for a foundation model. How do you see the interplay between these?

It's funny. One of the strategic decisions that we made for this company is to specifically not train our own models, to use off-the-shelf models only. And I think a lot of people today with AI are trying to figure out how to make their own particular fine-tuned model or specific to their particular area. And instead, we said, no, no, we're going to treat LLMs as commodity. I'm going to assume that GPT-4 level intelligence becomes cheap, efficient, and easy to access in open source. That's a pretty good assumption, I think, at this point. And so what we're trying to do, the way that we're approaching it is taking that as a given. What are some of the things that now become possible in the way you build software outside of that? The whole space of what also has been interesting this last year.12 months ago, it looks like the only game in town for GPT-4 level intelligence was OpenAI. And now that is absolutely not the picture at all. There's 20, 30 different models, including some open source ones, some of them which can run on a consumer machine that can give you a similar level of quality. That's just insane. Now that we know that these techniques work so well, helping tune them to get to squeeze more performance out of them is pretty significant. I have no expertise in that area. I just assume I'm watching all this amazing talent and capital swarming on this problem and trusting that this will continue to get significantly better and so far that has played out well.

SPEAKER_0: It's interesting. There's a feeling I get every time I go on Hugging Face and I download a model that I know cost them more than $100 million to train and can kind of go bananas with just this raw model. And that's the goal. I mean, the goal is to give these away and to keep.

I love doing these models as little holograms of human culture and intuition, these little things, these little cheap things you can plug into stuff. My friend Allison Gopnik is a child psychology professor at Berkeley, and she has this frame of AI stone soup. And so the stone soup story is, you know, someone comes, they make a soup out of just a stone. They put a stone into it. They say, ah, you know, what would make this better is a little bit of a garnish. So someone adds a garnish. They say, oh, you know, this is great, but you don't make it even better if you added a slightly bigger garnish. And before you know it, someone's put a chicken in. It's an amazing soup. And someone says, wow, it's amazing, this soup. And it just came from a stone. And that's the way I think that tech is about LLMs. People go, wow, this transformer algorithm and a bunch of compute produces this amazingly intelligent thing. And it's like, no, it's just, absorbing all of human culture that you threw into it. That is where most of the interesting upside comes from. Transformers do an insanely good job at extracting subtle patterns out of very large data sets. And it turns out that if you give them all of human intelligence that we thought to write down and replicate elsewhere, you get a really useful little thing that can reflect back. Really interesting things, you know?

SPEAKER_0: And it's wild to see. I mean, look on the biology side of it. You'll see models that are created just taking, like, a base text-to-image model, and you can throw in a sequence, like an RNA sequence, and then get an image out of the RNA folding pattern on it and get, like, remarkably good accuracy.

Insanely good. It feels like it's tapping into a secret structure of the universe. That if you were just patient enough at plowing through this, you could figure out. And the LLM and the training process and even its inference process is patient enough to do it.

SPEAKER_0: It's also interesting though because Not every space has the same volume of information that you can just kind of throw everything in and get it out. And, you know, we've talked before, I would build these models for my kids of like Batman and Iron Man and all these so that they can make superheroes. And one fun test was just to say, like, how does error propagate in these systems with smaller amounts of information? So if I take a fine tuning and I want to train it on like Adam West's Batman, and take 10 pictures of Adam West Batman, I can get 9 out of 10 pictures out that look like Adam West Batman. It's kind of impressively good. But if I change one of those pictures to a Batman Lego action figure, and then I change two of the 10, and then I change three of the 10, 100% of the output looks good from a distance. But then when you get a little bit closer to it, 100% of the output is super creepy and very, very bizarre.

The distinction of quality or rigor is the closer you get, the more compelling it gets. And AI-generated slop has exactly the inverse of that, right? Like, wow, it looks amazing. And the closer you look at them, you're like, what the heck? That doesn't make any sense. Like, that thing, like, there's a finger missing. Or I was watching some AI-generated videos. I was at a Thai restaurant in Berkeley a few weeks ago. And there's, like, a wall that was just a TV. It was playing AI-generated Christmas scenes. And they looked at the beginning. I was like. My lizard brain goes, pretty. And as I'm watching them, very distractingly as I'm trying to talk to somebody, you watch the videos that were generated. Watching AI-generated videos is a trip because the model is trying to make sense of each frame to frame and interpret it and try to make it feel coherent without a real true world model, per se. And so it sometimes just.changes its interpretation of the scene, and it will start evolving the video in a very different direction. So at one point, there was a scene above a little Christmas village, and the camera was coming in at it like this. And there was a little bokeh of the lens flare, kind of. And for some reason, at one point halfway through, it started interpreting the bokeh as big lanterns hanging in the sky. So now you started flying through these big orbs that were just hanging in the sky for some reason. It's a very uncanny experience watching the AI struggle to understand what's happening in that scene. So you get a bunch of these oddities the closer you are.

SPEAKER_0: It's very, very bizarre. And I want to thank you so much for coming out here. It's great to see you again in person. And a huge round of applause for Alex. We're going to bring you back up, I think, for questions at the end. So if you can just hang out and not go too far. And the next piece is actually going to build slightly on this. The thing I really find fascinating is high-quality information yields such amazingly good outcomes, and yet those little bits of noise cascade at such an extreme level. And it's a total privilege today to be able to talk about mTrust and to really start bringing this out publicly. It's something that Thomas and Harold and team have been working on for years, and we're at a really interesting point. And as I mentioned, as we look back at the history of our company, where we had such a strong piece of quality in physical goods and in materials, and how that drove science. Our goal is to bring this into the digital age. And with M-Trust, we've built an IP portfolio and a product set and now a development kit that can immutably link the physical and digital world. And if you think of blockchain as being cool because it's this immutable ledger, it falls apart when you try to connect it to something real. And we saw with the NFT craze a couple of years ago, you'd have art going for tens of millions of dollars and the way to show that it was. This piece of art attached to the NFT was a piece of hair that was epoxied in the art. But that's not truly a one-to-one piece with the painting. And what we do is we look at both the tags on the objects that are being read, taking something called hash entropy enhancement. So the hash being the cryptographic signature combined with the entropy or how difficult it is to remove from this object to be able to create this one-to-one match and then trace it across the process. And this enables some really interesting things, looking at product passports and traceability. So being able to take a drug or a reagent and say, this came from this specific batch, this batch was tested on this device, this device was last calibrated on this date using this certified reference material, and, and, and, to the back of it. But it also enables token economies and the ability for machines to be able to communicate with machines and have some sense of root of where things came from. An extraordinary effort, extraordinary lift, hugely complicated set of problems that the team solved in looking at the physical objects, the verification, the digital twin. And our goal is to help build resilient value chains, to boost agility and eliminate bottlenecks, to enable these tamper-proof digital twins, these tamper-proof equivalents of the physical version using these cryptographic anchors and establishing a layer of cyber-physical trust. And creating privacy-preserving digital ecosystems while safeguarding data. This matters because in order to have trust in AI, we need to know where the information came from that actually trained these models. And while a lot of the broad kind of generic LLMs have worked because they're across a broad corpus of publicly available information, most of the things that are critical for science and technology are not publicly available. And you need to know where this came from and what this was. It also allows the security of autonomous machines. So as we have full end-to-end processes, where the machines can communicate with one another. Machines are ruthlessly efficient at rooting out trust and statistical trust in the sense that this thing is actually this thing, much better than humans are. So getting it ready for a machine-to-machine world is critical, and also just to ensure data integrity and quality. It turns out, this might surprise people, building the blockchain backends is slightly complicated. One of the amazing things that the team did was take all of the infrastructure layer that was necessary to empower this from the Web3 infrastructure to the registries and tokens to digital twins and signing services, and make it so you do not need to be a blockchain expert to be able to build applications off of it. AND EVERYTHING THAT HAS COME OUT NOW IS THE TECHNOLOGY BRIDGE FROM THE PORTAL THAT BASICALLY ALLOWS YOU TO CONFIGURE WHATEVER TYPE OF ENVIRONMENT YOU WANT TO RUN WITH SETS OF READERS AND SETS OF TAGS AND AUTOMATICALLY TIE IT TO THE SECURE BACK END WITHOUT NEEDING TO BE A BLOCKCHAIN CODER. OBVIOUSLY THERE ARE THE APIs AND SDKS, THE SCANNING DEVICES, THIS ABILITY TO RAPIDLY DEPLOY THE TOOLS AND DATA INTEGRATION THAT MAKES IT USABLE REALLY ACROSS ANY APPLICATION. This is a quick look at the console. I don't think I'll go through the whole piece. But again, everything about this was made to reduce the need to do hardcore back-end coding, to distill it down to here's my line of code that I'm going to integrate into my application. And even to remove that layer completely just to auto-build applications on the fly. It was great to be able to talk to Alex here because machines are getting better and better at auto-building, essentially, these application sets. And so to be able to have this underlying tooling becomes really interesting. All the documentation, all the guides are there. There's mtrust.io. You can see everything you need around this. Creating the application, simple as adding a name. Adding permissions. Here's our registry. Setting your token. And creating the app. You don't want to lose the token. That's bad. But then you copy and paste the line into your code and it's set. It's very similar to how Stripe did financial processing to be able to embed into websites. So without needing to rebuild everything from scratch. We have two readers that we're starting out with, and this is what we're very excited to introduce today. We have the developer kit that we're starting to accept, essentially, applications for, for invites to be able to start building. They're over here. Thomas and Harold can give you guys demos. It is some of the coolest stuff that's there. Form Factor is fantastic. And the mTrust portal, which has all of the SDKs and APIs for folks to use. Really want to congratulate one the team for a heroic feat this has been I think 70 years in the making they have over 70 patents now that have been granted in a number of territories it's been just a great job and so huge round of applause for Thomas Harold the MTrust team everyone who's been it been a part of that extremely, extremely exciting to have this. And I'd like to introduce Laura to come up and have a quick discussion around this. Laura Matz is our Chief Science and Technology Officer as well as the CEO of Athenia. So welcome, Laura. Welcome, welcome.

SPEAKER_2: Very exciting and also my congratulations to the team. It's been a long time coming. And honestly, I think, James, what I wanted to do in our discussion is maybe we can go back to the picture of the yeah, maybe a chemist fair is a good one. Perfect. So I think a couple of things maybe we want to talk about in a little bit more detail. The first thing might be why Merck? Why Merck K&G, Armstadt, Germany? It maybe isn't obvious how we got here and why we thought this was a critical part of our journey. But I think you touched on it in the beginning of this history of sort of quality being in our DNA. And this now kind of coming full circle into the digital age. So maybe give a little more detail.

SPEAKER_0: And it's wild to go back, right? Because in 1868, we're the oldest pharma company in the world. But what was pharma at that time? These were pharmacies, right? Where you had gardens behind the pharmacy and you would go out and you'd pick your stuff and kind of collect it and get it together. And the real radical changes occurred when you started productionizing the creation of the individual components that you could ship out in bottles to different places. I mean, you needed to know what was there, right? And this simple guarantee was obviously just a, it was a trust me letter, but it's wild to me that that was only 170 something years ago is the first time we have at least some sense that I'm assuring you that this thing is this thing. I'm going to take some liability for that and have some guarantee behind it. And yet, it is such a critical part of the scientific method and the scientific reproducibility at large. But I think for us, it's always been about not resting on your laurels, right? Because that was a trust me letter. The company spent the next hundred years hiring the process engineers and literally writing the book on what it takes to ensure quality in the industrialization of chemicals. And I think this is a very natural evolution. The output of a scientific experiment is information. And creating these tiebacks and the traceability back to this data point came from an actual experiment that was physically done somewhere versus something that was just generated in the ether, I think becomes really existentially important for us.

SPEAKER_2: So one of the things, that was actually a good lead-in to the next question I had. So as you think about this technology, one of the fundamental shifts it makes is how we do quality assurance. And how we verify The product is the product, and it has the right signature. And today, while we generate the data, it's in a digital format. There's still a person that downloads that data, reviews it, and then submits it into another database to verify, yes, this is the right data. I guess maybe can you describe how that changes with mTrust? Because I think this is fundamental to how the world will evolve in many different industries over the next five, 10 years.

SPEAKER_0: It's a lot of trust me letters. They're more formal, and we have more processes that we randomly audit. And I think it's almost a tightening of these.these boundaries around it, the sense that it's not a randomized audit, it's kind of an audit on demand at any point in time. I think the other thing is as we look at where things are headed overall, it's not like I'm gonna have a batch of medicine that is created for lots and lots of people, right? Where I can give you the same batch that I can give you, right? If I have a medicine that is coming off of a sample of my specific biopsy that is gonna have some specific sequence and then the resultant treatment is specifically for that sample, I really, really want to be sure that the treatment I'm getting is that one. It's not something different. These become life or death. The failure risk is like, the cost of failure is very high.

SPEAKER_2: So one thing I think, as we've kind of worked on the preparation for the launch and all the work that the team's done to get this ready, I think one thing we've realized is how many different industries this can serve. So of course, for us, it can enable quality in electronics materials. It can enable quality in drugs, in life science. But there's also other industries that it can serve. And I think that's one of the fundamental things we're trying to do is launch this so that many industries can take advantage of the technology. So maybe give a little bit of background in terms of the broader industries that this can make an impact to, and how do we want to engage those industries?

SPEAKER_0: First admitting that we're not the experts in those industries, right? And so a lot of our time has been spent on how do we not have to do the lift to be able to get these in other sectors where it matters. I mean, food is a great example. We have the chicken go on tour with us for many discussions for many, many years. But it is a fantastic example of scale and the need to have some kind of traceability back to say, where did this come from? And if something goes wrong, how can I have proper recalls? And how can I have all of the kinds of regular regulatory sides of this in place. Anywhere where your cost of failure is high, I think, is the earliest adopter. But again, our push is not to be like the purveyor of quality necessarily as much as the enabler of quality. How do we create the mechanism so that folks can add quality and traceability across? And just to give you a sense of how core this is to our business, if you look at the life science side of our business, we have hundreds of thousands of products. We have 50-something different versions of salt, sodium chloride. Why? Because some people need it 90% pure, some need it 97%, some need it 99%, some need it 99%, some need it 99.999%, plus whatever additional tests that go off of it. This isn't the only space that that matters. And especially, Alex was talking about the videos. As you look at these generated videos, you're like, something seems off here. That feeling of it feeling off is the uncanny valley. The sense of what is it between real and fake? This generated thing that you know is not there and that uneasiness you have as you kind of wade through it. And I think especially now, we need some mechanism to navigate this. We need some root and map to say this thing is actually from here. This thing is actually real; we can trust right?

SPEAKER_2: So, I mean having worked in the quality importance of having worked on the importance of quality and electronic materials. I'm personally excited about this journey because I have worked through the standard verification, the verification of analytical data, to be able to really verify and know that yes, this data is the right data for this batch. And to be able to directly digitally transfer that to our customers, I think will be really, really enabling. But for me, what's also interesting is there are so many other industries like aerospace, food-as an example. And I think personally, my daughter will be very excited to know that we can now verify the chicken that she's eating is from a sustainable farm. And so there's many different opportunities here and many different customer bases. But I think the exciting part that we're trying to do today with the launch of mTrust is to really sort of let that go into many different industries and really sort of seed the technology so that it can enable quality digitally for many different products. And I think maybe one thing to kind of close, then we'll take some questions. James, maybe give a little bit of the history of the journey. How did we get here and what did it take to kind of bring this to life? Because honestly, when you see the readers, it seems so easy, I think. It seems like an iPhone 4.

SPEAKER_0: Yeah. I mean, the journey started in 2016, I want to say, after Sigma Aldrich was acquired. And we were trying to look kind of across these businesses that on the surface don't seem that interrelated, right? You have electronics, you have life science, and you have pharma. And then you have a pandemic that hits and suddenly it totally makes sense when everyone is glued to devices and you need science to kind of get you out of it. You know, we had a set of lighthouses that we wanted to look at and kind of key bets that we wanted to make. And this really originated as one of those bets, the sense that we are going to need better mechanisms of validating that something is this thing. And you can see it from the trend side. And the good news is, we had wildly persistent people who are incredibly smart and incredibly focused on getting this done. And the team has just done an extraordinary job in pushing this through.

SPEAKER_2: So with that, maybe we would be happy to take any questions as well, I think. And Alex, please come up here.

I think a default feature for AI, people talk about AGI or whatever. I'm actually not worried about that. I think we're seeing clear topping out of models as we kind of run out of original material to train them on. But I worry that AI is going to lead to a very same-y culture of just everything just being boring and centrist and the same. And my hope is that it unleashes creativity. It allows people to be more expressive, and collaborative, and more authentic. But I don't think that feature is the default. I think that we have to work to make that happen. And I find, you know, People-when they use LLMs in an uninspired way, get the most uninspired slop back. When you use them in a creative way, you can go much further than you would have gone before. And my worry is it's just easier to do the former.

SPEAKER_0: It makes me think. I would joke: coming to CES for five years from 2015 on, it was a sequence of slightly thinner TVs with slightly thinner. You know, cables that went to them. And there was an exciting part here that we now have kind of like a new possible set of form factors that can emerge. But it is going to be fascinating to see like, does this lead to convergence? Because like, that's what it is optimized to converge, right? At every point.

And I think if LLMs are truly a disruptive technology, and they seem to me to be disruptive in the same way of like electricity being disruptive, it takes a long time to figure out what to do with that. And so I think people are like, oh, we've figured out what to do with AI. No, we have not at all. We're like in the early innings. And I think that the people who try to make sense of what you can do with this stuff, I was telling James earlier, a friend of mine at one of the former companies I was at said they were inspired by me leaving to go do an AI startup and that they had taken a sabbatical to learn about AI as an engineer. And I said, what did you do? And he said, well, it took three months and a ton of effort. And I got a little toy transformer running on my machine. And I said, that is the least interesting thing you could have done because you just take this for granted that these exist. The question is what to do with them. What can you poke at? What can you get them to do? And that's something that even the people who created them can't tell you. And I think that there's a, we're just at the early stage of figuring out what you can do with this. I think people, I know a number of people have told me that LLMs are definitely without question useless. Anyone who's ever said that statement has not clearly hasn't used them a lot because the idea that these will not change something large in society is impossible to me. And they're just so obviously, we just don't yet know what the best use of them is, you know?

SPEAKER_3: I have a question. So it worries me that we can't trace back our chickens right now with the technology. So what is the biggest differentiator that you see for the technology and what the competitive advantage that this is going to give the marketplace and those that adopted and are early adopters?

SPEAKER_0: I think at each level of additional quality and additional ability to accept liability, there's value that's there. It's easy to dismiss it as an anti-counterfeit play, and I think what's really unique is that it's take the concept of anti-counterfeit across every step of a process. And like what, what if I double-checked and triple-checked and quadruple-checked at every single step of an entire process and not only that, I'm checking the devices that are involved in each step of this process to ensure sure that it's working, it's that that next level up; it's I really i like thinking of it as an audit on demand because it's it's the ability to fully audit across everything in the value chain that happened, and uh we're certainly part of a business that additional quality yields additional value, but I feel like we're not alone in that. If you can't train an LLM on data past the end of 2022, as simple as that, there's a lot of value that's going to need to be there.

SPEAKER_2: One thing I'll add to what James said, I think that the team has spent a lot of time over the past two years making the technology pretty very straightforward to use, very easily adopted. You can see you can see here actually in the demos. You basically turn the reader on and it's ready to go, and it connects to your phone so that ease of use I think is something that will be differentiating and kind of enabling to proliferate right because. For this technology to take hold and for it to be usable, it has to be broadly applicable so that you can get traceability from the lab and individual analytical equipment. But then, also, you'd like to know, where did that material go in the warehouse? And you want it on scanners that can basically verify, yes, now it's in the warehouse. What's the shelf life? How long has it been there? So that sort of ease of use and kind of transparency across the entire value chain. I think it's something that we tried to design in so that when we launch, when we were at this point, it's basically able to be completely proliferated versus being niched in just one area.

SPEAKER_0: I also look at things like Lego Batman sounds really silly. I acknowledge that. But the thing I find really interesting about that is just a thought experiment also is biology is 30% error. Proteins misfold 30% of the time. If you take something where you infuse 30% error and noise into it, and you have 100% error of output, that is kind of terrifying, right? And how you reduce that so that these generative builds on whatever was there before doesn't lead to this 100% error, I think is just super critical.

SPEAKER_2: OK, well. Thank you, everyone, for joining us. This has been a journey that we've been on for since 2016. I didn't realize it was quite that long. But the past seven years have been really focused. And we're really excited to see now where the technology can go and what impact it can make. So thank you to the team. Alex, thank you so much for joining. A fascinating discussion. And honestly, you gave me a perspective on LLMs I didn't necessarily have before, but now I have something new to worry about, different than what I was worried about. And James, of course, you've been an incredible leader in this journey, so thank you. Thank you so much. Thank you, everyone.

messages.content: SPEAKER_1: Welcome back to Google Developers Live at I. O.from Moscone Center here in San Francisco. We're here to talk about Blink. I'm Louis Gray, and I'm joined by Alex Komorowski and Paul Irish. Welcome, gentlemen. Thanks for joining us. Absolutely. Real quickly, you know, we want to talk about Blink, a brand-new rendering engine. I want to give you guys a take on what's been introduced.
So Blink is a new rendering engine. It's based off of, originally off of WebKit. It's a rendering engine that's open source and it's used in Chromium and Chrome.

SPEAKER_1: So you said open source. Is it a Google product? Open source? Help us out.

SPEAKER_2: It's operated within the Chromium open source community. So there's Chromium, which is the open source implementation of Google Chrome. And they're basically 99% the same with a few very small differences. But Blink is an open source project, and likely even going to be, we expect it to be used in Opera's products as well.

So it's open source and one of the things that we really, one of our main goals with the project is to be as transparent and as open as possible. So we're trying to have as much of a discussion in public channels. We've already seen a lot of really good engagement from people from other companies, from other organizations, and web developers on these threads, sort of providing their feedback about features we're thinking about implementing, and it's been really great.
SPEAKER_1: So how does a new rendering engine benefit consumers anyway? Is it going to get faster? What's going to happen?

So as it starts out, it's based off of WebKit. So it's basically about as of a month ago, it was almost exactly the same. Since then, we've been optimizing some pieces on our side. And it will continue to get faster. And over time, however, a stronger, faster rendering engine allows you to do new things, so it allows developers to build better experiences. It allows them to be faster, so consumers'web pages will just appear faster and do more things.

SPEAKER_2: Like, one of the main things that we'd like to, you know, that we focus a lot on is speed, right? And so when I'm scrolling a page on my phone, I want it to stick to my finger, and I want that experience to be fantastic. And with Blink, we have the opportunity to make some really kind of radical changes to the infrastructure of how a browser is put together to really deliver a fast and awesome user experience.

SPEAKER_1: So one thing that's really interesting here, you look at the web. And it was talked about today in today's keynote. We're on the 20th anniversary of the introduction of the web. And one of the things that's really helped the web grow and just work on practically every device is standards. And so, with the introduction of a new rendering engine, is this a situation where web developers and app developers who code for the web are going to have to make two versions of their product? What does that mean for a developer side?

SPEAKER_2: There's no big real changes. Blink is dedicated to just supporting an interoperable web. And that's something that we believe a lot. And it's having a compatible interoperable web. Developers building for the web, you build web standards, you build just as you did, and you can expect it to work.

And I think back many, many years ago, it was different browsers were very different. I think in the past few years, The different rendering engines have gotten closer and closer. A lot closer. A lot closer, as everyone really is taking standards very seriously. We're also investing, we're very excited to invest more in testing. So helping write tests with standards organizations and other browser vendors that all the browser rendering engines can run. And that helps verify that these things are interoperable. And so, the developers only have to worry, they don't have to write totally different versions for different browsers.

SPEAKER_2: In the past, developers knew about browser quirks and things like that. And over the past few years, they've been less and less because browsers are running the exact same tests, even though they're different products.

SPEAKER_1: So this is a situation where you had modern browsers and non-modern browsers. Is Blink a cut above? Is it going to be the most modern browser? It certainly moves fast.

SPEAKER_2: And it gets new features all the time. But it's really you're going to get a consistent experience when you're coding to the new features.

Although the goal is to build the best possible rendering engine we can, but what's great about the open web is you have all these different compatible rendering engines. And so this is very healthy competition to spur innovation throughout the ecosystem.

SPEAKER_1: So one thing we've talked about before, since Blink is still fairly new, and we're talking a little bit about the new features that are going to come out, we expect the community to respond really strongly. One of the questions I have around browsing, is we have so many people who are browsing on mobile these days relative to the desktop. How much did the advancement of mobile usage of browsing impact the decision to look toward Blink? And do you think that mobile users can see the benefits of Blink as quickly as desktop?

I think if you saw the keynote this morning, Linus talked a lot about how the future of the web is mobile. And so we take this really seriously on the Chrome team. Now, the reason that we started Blink actually came down to the fact that Chromium's multi-process architecture is just different than the other WebKit-based browsers. And it led to complexity in the code base. And that was primarily the thing that drove us to make this decision. But yes, we care very deeply about mobile. And we're excited about the different kinds of things we can experiment with.

SPEAKER_1: RETO MEIER-And when you look at the complexity in the code base, I remember when we launched, we had our initial blog post and the reactions to it. A lot of discussion was taken to kind of getting away some of the cruft or older code that was available in WebKit. How does that benefit folks as well? Is it just easier to develop on it with fewer lines of code? And what are we getting away from?

I want to point out, actually, that WebKit is an amazing code base. And it's an amazing community. And what we were trying to remove were pieces that just weren’t relevant to Chromium. And so when you have extra stuff in a code base that you aren’t using, that’s code that has more surface area for bugs. It takes longer to check out as a developer. It takes longer to build, even. And so it was removing pieces that didn’t apply to Chromium but were still very nice pieces of code, like JavaScript Core, for example.

SPEAKER_1: And one of the things, I mean, you mentioned code and cruft. One of the fun things I’ve seen bump up in coverage of Blink is vendor prefixes. For those of us online who don’t know what a vendor prefix is, what is it, Paul? And why do I care about a vendor prefix?

SPEAKER_2: PAUL IRISHMANNENBURG-So a vendor prefix, the idea behind vendor prefix is a way to insulate users when browsers want to introduce a new experimental feature. And they want to be able to have a little bit of freedom to maybe change it in the future. And then browser vendors will work together to standardize it. And then the prefix that's in front of it will disappear. It will graduate. Now, that was kind of the idea behind it, and it sounds good. But in practice, over the past few years, it's kind of not worked out. And it's had some kind of harmful effects on the ecosystem. So one of the things with the introduction of Blink was that we were able to take a policy and said that we're not going to be introducing any new vendor prefixes. It's painful as a developer to deal with having multiple vendor prefixes, and we're not going to add any new ones, and we're not going to introduce new features that have this. And this is And this is a similar model to what Mozilla with Firefox has gone towards, and also the W3C CSS Working Group has kind of said that this is the direction that makes sense going forward.

SPEAKER_1: Now, Blink is still fairly new. I know a lot of people often when something comes out right away, they say, how can I get my hands on it? And because Blink is an engine, and not an app, it's not like you can just download Blink and start playing. So if you want to get your hands on it, either as a developer or as a user, what's the best way to do that?

Alex Komoroske-So again, from the very beginning, it was literally just WebKit. And then we've been developing in a slightly different direction. And so all the newest versions ever since April 4, I think, when we announced Blink, have been using Blink. It's just that it's not really that different yet. It's getting more and more different. And so those versions will become on the stable channel of Chrome in a number of weeks.

SPEAKER_2: ALEX KOMOROSKE-Yeah, the Chrome beta has Blink in it. And Canary has Blink in it. You know, try it out.

SPEAKER_1: It's not going to feel very different. And as a Chrome OS user, do I get access to Blink as well, or is that a separate branch? So, I mean, I'm using my Pixel here. I use Pixel practically every single day as often as I can. And so one of the things that's been really cool for me for the Pixel, I know we have a conversation about that tomorrow, is you're finding more and more web apps are replacing the things that I traditionally had to use a desktop for. Is that part of also our consideration as we build a new rendering engine to enable what's next in the world of web apps? Absolutely.

We believe very firmly that the web platform deserves to be as powerful as any other native platform. And so we've been pushing within the ecosystem, within the standards bodies, and really supporting that drive to make the web platform as powerful as possible.

SPEAKER_1: Well, Paul and Alex, I appreciate you guys coming down today. I know you have a very busy Google I/O., really one of the hottest topics that's out there for the web and for Google in general. For those of you on the live stream, stay tuned. We have four more sessions coming your way, whether you're on the Android channel, Chrome channel, or the technical ones as well. And we'll be back right after those with more programming at Google Developers Live at I. O.

source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/transcripts/transcript-Google Developers Live at I_O 2013 - Blink and Your Browser.m4a_spaced.txt

messages.content: ALEX KOMOROWSKI-Hi, welcome. So we're going to get started before we're all full in this room, just for the live stream. I'm Alex Komorowski. I'm lead product manager on Chrome's web platform team.
SPEAKER_1: And hi, my name is Elizabeth Morante, and I am the product manager for Chrome Security.

ALEX KOMOROWSKI-So today we're going to tell you about four forces, some of them old, some of them new, that together combine to create the next generation mobile web. Now, a lot of the times at I. O., we tell you about all kinds of amazing features that are going to come six months, maybe a year from now. But today, we're going to cover only things that are already shipped in Chrome that you can use. So we don't have that much time, so we'll cover things at a pretty high level. But there's lots of great dev bytes and box talks here at I. O.that go into more depth on all of these. So let's get started. So the reason that Elizabeth and I are here is because we love Chrome.
SPEAKER_1: And we love Chrome because, like many of you, we love the web.

I mean, it's kind of amazing that you can connect with any topic or community you want without asking anyone permission, which allows all kinds of kind of wacky stuff to happen. So my personal favorite is this site called Real Ultimate Power. Do I have any idea what that is? Okay, yeah, there's a few of you out there. So this is this amazing satire site. This guy has a page about ninjas. He's super crazy into ninjas, and he hates pirates. Oh, of course. That's important. But he just put this site up there. People thought it was hilarious. But if you go to that site in a browser that does support push notifications, maybe they add another button that says, hey, I want to be notified when this thing changes. And then if I, as a user, opt in, I can have a really high-value thing that's really important to me even before it's shipped in every browser out there. So I think that's pretty exciting.
SPEAKER_1: So we talked about a lot of topics at a high level today, but there are a number of DevBytes and BoxTalks that you can visit that will go into a lot of these topics more in depth.
So today we talked about three things.
SPEAKER_1: The superpower of the web, breach.
How performance has made huge strides on mobile web.
SPEAKER_1: And how we're making powerful new ways for you to build relationships with your users. Many available now and a lot more coming soon.
So the three of these alone aren't that special. It's the confluence of these three things that's really important.
SPEAKER_1: Well, you're forgetting the most important one.

That's a good point. Because these things by themselves, they just make it possible to do cool things. But what happens when you combine that with this global community of tinkerers and builders and makers, well, that's when something really awe-inspiring happens.
SPEAKER_1: We can't wait to see what you build. Thanks.
So I think if there are any live questions, we can take them here.

SPEAKER_4: First of all, fantastic talk. Thank you. The question is about the service workers which respond to events. Does it involve the events like Ajax requests, or it's only when you don't have a network which initializes during the event?

So basically, what happens is you get a fetch event. So in your service worker, you say, Chrome, please notify me when any content from my domain is fetched. And then you'll catch that event. You'll pass in what the request URL was. And you can respond to it that way. And that happens even when you're fully connected. Because, of course, you might be on flaky connectivity. And so you want to make sure you can design your experience with total control over what happens over networking to your site.
SPEAKER_4: You mean content like HTML or it includes AJAX requests?
Basically, almost every request that goes to your site. There's a few exceptions for specific things. All right.

SPEAKER_4: So if you're using things like OAuth, which automatically authenticates, what's the design pattern to get data? Like, for example, if you used Google Plus authentication, right, which directly logs on to your I think that's a great question to go into more depth in the office hours.
But, like, yeah, you have total control over this. It's pretty powerful.

SPEAKER_8: I got an easy one for you. Okay. I hope. The home screen, you mentioned that you find it interesting how many people use the home screen. I'm shocked at that. Where can I see the numbers for it? How many people actually add stuff to the home screen?

So I don't know if we have those numbers to share today, but it's one of those things that even despite being a buried feature, I think people find a lot of value. And that's one of the reasons that we decided to bring this feature to other websites in a more proactive way when we feel pretty confident that the user will be interested in that one.
SPEAKER_8: And where could I find those numbers? Where do you find them from?
Those are numbers that we have. Oh, secret numbers.

SPEAKER_3: What's the advantage of using a service worker over something like what we had at Angry Birds like two or three years ago? At Google I, two or three years ago, they were using offline storage for Angry Birds. What's the advantage of using service worker?

So service worker allows you to have very fine-grained control over all aspects of what's happening on the network. So then you can use technologies like IndexedDB and explicit cache API to do stuff. Whereas before, you were limited to a very high-level declarative approach. And if it worked for you, it was great. But more likely, it didn't work for you. It couldn't do exactly what you wanted to handle in this particular case. And so it was basically useless. So ServiceWorker, the way to think of it is, are you familiar with the extensible web manifesto? So that's a notion that we should be defining the lower-level primitives that explain how the web platform works. So getting rid of the high-level magic and giving developers access to the low-level primitives that make that magic work. So Service Worker is like digging down deep below Abcache to figure out what was there and how it worked and expose that to developers. Does that make sense? Probably not. Sorry.

SPEAKER_2: I have a question on the User experience on the offline sites that you showed me. So let's say the user is traveling inside a tunnel and There's like a network break. How do we notify the user like At this moment you are online and then you go offline and this Experience that you are having is the offline period, and then the network can come back again, and it can come online again.

That's great. And so the cool thing about Service Worker is you have full control to do what's right for your app. So in particular, for example, I think the IO Web App does this. If you're on flaky connectivity, it can say, 'I'm just going to default and show everything directly from the cache, and then fetch resources, fresh resources.'And so what might happen is the user's already looking at it. Fresh content has come back. You don't want to go and put the content there, because they'll lose their train of thought. But with Service Worker and some of these demos, what people do is they'll have a small bit of UI that says, 'fresh content is available if you scroll up.'And that way, you don't lose your way in the app. But the point is, of course, that you have control to do what's right for your app, which is pretty exciting.

SPEAKER_2: Oh, that's cool.

SPEAKER_5: Thank you. Hi. My question is, in the demo that you showed, I get a notification from the Guardian app, and you go to the home screen of the Guardian app icon, and then you click, and then you open, and then you read. Is it possible that from the notification directly I can?

SPEAKER_1: Yeah, that was just a bug in the demo.

So what happens is when you tap on the notification, your service worker receives an event that says the notification was clicked. At that point, you can say, okay, do nothing, or you can say focus a tab that existed before or open a new tab.

SPEAKER_1: Yeah, so I was supposed to click on the notification and jump directly to the content that it was notifying me about.

SPEAKER_5: Okay, sure, thank you.

SPEAKER_6: Hi. Very similar question, but rather than tapping on a notification, what if you get an email with a link? Is there any kind of association that would open up that installed web app?

So I don't know the particulars of exactly how it works, but if you have an installed web app with a manifest, there will be some cases that don't necessarily fit exactly perfectly into the intent system of Android, but we're working on those. I don't have all the information for you in depth on that, though, right? We should take the office hours. Sure.

SPEAKER_7: One question. Okay. Are the notification preferences synced across a user's account, or is it per device?

SPEAKER_1: It's per device.

And the reason for that is the context that you opt in is very device-specific. So I might be interested in seeing celebrity gossip on my personal machine, but I don’t actually want, I don’t care about it enough to be notified when I’m on the go. And so exactly where the right user contacts depends on the device you’re in, and so it’s not synced.
SPEAKER_7: And is that notification preference per domain or per specific website?

It’s per domain, basically. And so the idea is you want to make sure as a site author that you use it very respectfully. So one thing we didn’t show in this is the notifications that show up have a very obvious site settings button. And if a user taps on that, they're taken directly into Chrome settings for that domain where it's one or a couple more taps to revoke permissions for that domain. And so as a site author, you want to make sure that you explain to users what you're going to do and that you respect their attention because it's very, very easy for them. Of course, they are in control.to revoke permission very easily if you abuse it.
SPEAKER_7: Thank you.
Sure. Thank you so much.
SPEAKER_7: Appreciate it.
