messages.content: SPEAKER_1: Hello, this is Google I. O., and this is Coffee with a Googler. I am sitting on a chair, but that doesn't really matter, because on the sofa next to me is Alex Komorowski. Having a good time? Are you enjoying?
I'm having an amazing time. There's all kinds of; every year it's different. And so they have all these interesting like swing sets and stuff. It's pretty cool.
SPEAKER_1: So what's your job title then?
I am the product management lead on Chrome's web platform team.

SPEAKER_1: Okay, that's quite long. So I kind of think of you as a sort of web hero or champion of the web. Oh, okay, sure. Yeah, I like that. That sounds good. Let's go with that. So why the web? What is the web to you?

So I think like a lot of people have had this long, deep connection with the web. So I shared yesterday's story, this embarrassing story about the real ultimate power and actually how we uploaded a picture of our crew team posing for that. But for me, it actually really became sort of an important moral issue for me in college when I read this book, The Wealth of Networks, by Yochai Benkler, who's a professor at Yale or something. And it totally sold me on the power for society, these open systems or whatever. But all of this, I guess, is really abstract. And so, I think one of the reasons I love the web is that it makes it really easy for you to reach a lot of people with your stuff and get your ideas out there.

SPEAKER_1: Yeah, and part of it for me as well, I guess, is that and the openness of it. One of the things that really got me into the web was like, view source. I was able to go and see, so wow, this is a thing on the web, view source. Wow, that's how it's been put together.

Yeah, and it's great for tinkerers because you don't need to get anyone's permission. You don't need to buy anything. You can just use a text editor that comes on your computer and get into it that way. That's totally how I started. That's how I bet a lot of us started on it.

SPEAKER_1: Yeah, I know we have a bit of a sort of back and forth or banter with the Android guys. And the Hello World for Android comes in at like 800K, I think they've got down to. Whereas Hello World on the web is Hello World, right? It's just a text file with that. Upload that somewhere and off you go. So you gave a talk yesterday morning on the next generation of the mobile web.
Yep, that's right.
SPEAKER_1: I mean, I remember Web 2.0 from a few years ago. It was a kind of marketing thing with no substance.

Well, so actually I found that for those kinds of things, the terms, when they're really successful, some things are just always hot air, right? They start as hot air, they end as hot air. But some things that are actually really something meaningful then become so meaningful that everyone who doesn't understand them starts using those words. So, like, Ajax. Oh, my God. Yes. Ajax was totally a thing, right? It totally meant something very specific. And then all these CEOs and marketing people said, oh, yeah, like, you know, Ajax. Can we make it Ajax? Cool web things. So it lost its meaning. And so, meanwhile, we're sitting here like. That's not Ajax. What does that mean? That's silly.

SPEAKER_1: Yeah, so it's like, yes, it's asynchronous. Yes, it's JavaScript. We can go with that. It's not XML. It was very rarely XML. And it all sort of fell down from there. It just became to mean anything vaguely asynchronous.

But to me, Web 2.0 is the same way. Yeah, it's a buzzword. It totally has become a buzzword. But the fact of the matter is there's a number of amazingly successful companies that got their start during that time. And so now we look back on it and we're like, oh, yeah, Web 2.0, whatever.

SPEAKER_1: Always a really successful companies that we totally take for granted today, totally got their start because of the web, yeah, and because companies were asking for can we make this the second web rather than the first web. I was like, oh, yeah, yeah. So what you essentially mean there is you want it to be a bit faster and a bit simpler. And that was a good excuse, I think, for us as developers to sort of crowbar some of what we wanted.

Absolutely, because I think with the earlier web, everyone had this like, oh, it's static sites. Things are slow. You click. It takes a while to load. And there really wasn't any reason at that point, because techniques like AJAX and XML HTTP request allowed you to build these immersive things. And so actually, a new label really helped developers go with it and find that. In the same way, I think, as what we're at now, people say, 'Oh my gosh, look, this native app loads so much faster.'It's like, well, look at the web's version. It just took this huge 25 megs required to download it. It's got 13 different third-party scripts, many of which are calculating the same stuff with synchronous layouts on every scroll event. Of course, that's not fast.
SPEAKER_1: So how is the next generation of the web different?

I think it's people really learning how to use some of these new technologies, how to build things that feel at home on mobile. So using the Rails framework that we talked about to help. Understand when you have good performance and when you should be optimizing; when you add an extra script to your site and go, nope, you know what? That made my site load too slow or scroll too slowly. I'm not going to include it. It's not worth it.

SPEAKER_1: That's one of the things I'm hearing sort of becoming more and more popular and it's music to my ears is the idea of having a performance budget as part of your design process. And that's a big part of Rails.

It totally is because if you wait to the end and try to make it highly performant, it's too late. So what you really want to do is build something fast from the beginning. And as you layer in things, make sure you don't ever regress your performance. That's one of the reasons that we're looking forward to doing things in DevTools, to make it easier. So right now, when you open up DevTools, you're overwhelmed. And there's so much there. And that's why we're looking into tools that will make it much more; hey, we looked at your page. Your reaction time is pretty good. Load time could be better. Animation speed, terrible. Like, you are missing, you are always janking. This third-party script right here is doing way too much work on every scroll event. Take it out. And that would make it much easier for you to go, oh, sure, OK.
SPEAKER_1: Yeah, because the web is fast by default, right? I mean, if you have a blank page, pretty fast. It's all regression. Right, exactly.

And I think also, by the way, everyone goes, well, native apps load much faster. Well, again. Well-made native apps definitely can load very quickly. Well-made web apps can as well. But remember, you're comparing the first visit to a website. You're downloading all the images and resources to display this on the screen versus a thing where when you install the native app, it did take a while to download. In fact, it probably took a minute or so to download. It's just you did it at install time.
SPEAKER_1: Yeah, it's many megabytes. And if you're on 3G, it's normally like, I'm going to have to download this later.
And it gets past that 10-second mark. So you say, yeah, I'll install this. You know, I'm going to go over and play Solitaire or something.

SPEAKER_1: I'm data roaming at the moment, and I'm locked down to 3G, so I'm completely aware of that right now. So it's not just about the improvements to the next generation web, but we're getting new features as well, right?

Absolutely. So one of the ones we're most excited about is push notifications. So on mobile in particular, increasingly we're moving from this world where it's driven by the grid of apps and into more things that happen in context. So actually, just before we started this, I got a notification on my watch about some breaking news that happened from the ice. And I was able to interject it in conversation because I just glanced at my watch. That actually came from our website, which is really cool.
SPEAKER_1: So even though it's on your watch, which I mean, we don't run Chrome on the watches, but it's.
So Chrome running on my Android device, got the notification, and then it's just a normal system notification, so it showed up on my watch.

SPEAKER_1: PAUL LEWISOHNKOWICZ-It's brilliant. So I get sent a link by someone. I'll go to that website. And one of the things I like about the web is I don't have to think, 'ooh, do I trust this website'before clicking a link. But to be able to do notifications and things, there's code running in the background. So what kind of protection? Is there a break in the security of the web here?

That is a really critical point. And everyone goes, 'ah, you should just copy native apps.'Native apps do some things extremely well. The web, one of its biggest and most important strengths is how easy it is for people to navigate to a diverse collection of stuff so easily. So, if you were afraid to tap links, that would be a total failure. Right? Because that's the benefit of the web. In fact, yesterday we shared that, on average, Chrome for Android users visit 100 distinct domains a month on average, which is crazy.
SPEAKER_1: LAURENCE MORONEY-This is more than most people have apps.

DAN GALPIN-Oh, way over. And it's because it's so easy for you to dive into these things. So that's incredibly important for us to maintain. So the way that it works is: you visit a site. And what we encourage developers to do is, in context, explain to users why they might want push notifications. So, say for example, you visit the Guardian. You've read multiple articles on a given topic. And they say, hey, at the end, you like this topic. Would you like to be notified when there's more articles on this topic? Well, yes. Yes, I would. You tap that on. And then, of course, Chrome verifies, hey, Guardian would love to send you notifications, just to make sure that you knew what you were doing. And at that point, if you say yes, they'll allow them to register for notifications. There's an important bit, too, that says, then say you register. And it says, this sounds good. You register. And they send you a notification every minute. We also make it very easy in each notification we show. There's a prominent 'Site settings'button. If you tap that, it's just a couple more taps to turn off notifications for that domain to make it very easy for you to revoke that permission if you decide that that site wasn't using it in a respectful way.

SPEAKER_1: That's excellent as well because we've been building a few test apps with this technology. And just because there's just a button that sends a push message to someone else, I've sort of woken up in the morning with like 100 of these. And it's just so good that it's like, right, actually. I'm done. I'm done with this. We've had our fun. And yeah, we're sort of back to safety, able to just revoke those permissions. And you're sort of back to just being like on any other website.

And I think that's what's so important about this feature is everyone goes, 'yeah, you've got push notifications and apps.'But remember, websites, you visit so many of them, so easy to stumble across other things. And instead of having this all or nothing, do you want to fully commit to this thing, give it space on your home screen, give it all these powerful permissions? Nah, I just want push notifications. Like, you know, I check into this airline. I don't find them very often. I don't want their app, necessarily. I don't find them that often. But it says, do you want to be notified if your gate changes? Why, yes. Yes, I would.
SPEAKER_1: Yes. Yeah, exactly that case. It's not something that you would visit every day. But that notification is so important to you. Exactly. It's critical.

And so I think we're combining this amazing reach, this no-commitment for the web, and allowing you as a user to sort of escalate on your own terms as you feel comfortable and pick and choose which aspects of the experience you want.

SPEAKER_1: So the whole push messaging thing is one of the features that's from Native. When I think of the apps on my home screen, the native apps, the reason they, well, previous to now, couldn't have been done on the web is push notifications. As the web, are we just copying native? Are we always going to be behind and just picking and choosing their innovations?

I don't think it would be a good way for us to go. And so, yes, we look at the features, the things that people are relying on these devices, and we figure out a way to bring it responsibly to the web. But at every point, we're trying to marry these capabilities with the power of the web, this reach. And so, for example, background sync is a functionality that native apps often have. They can sort of wake up in the background, refresh in their resources. Why don't we just bring this to the web? Well, it's actually really hard. So imagine that you visit a site. Every day on your personal computer, it's an embarrassing celebrity gossip site that you don't want to admit to anybody you visit. You go off and you never would visit that at work because you're supposed to be working. So you visit it and we say, okay, cool. You visit this pretty often. Let's let your background sync that. Well, now you go to your work network.and it syncs in the background, and your boss goes, Jake, what are you doing? Why are you wasting time on the company network? So that's just, we have to think very carefully about these things and make sure that users have the right expectations about what's happening so the right things can happen and they don't inadvertently waste their battery or leak personal information or what have you.

SPEAKER_1: And it made me really happy in the keynote to hear that the Android's permissioning model; they're changing to what you mentioned before, this kind of just-in-time contextual permissioning model. So I thought that's a case where, hey, they've kind of copied the success of the web, which I thought was great.

TIMOTHY JORDAN-But I think there's a lot of great properties of apps and of the way the web works. And I think it's great for them to be inspired by one another, to figure out how to bring these lessons from the other realm into the other and just make it better for everybody.

SPEAKER_1: web progressing and getting all of these new features, but still staying true to the benefits of the web. This is Mr. Alex Komorowski talking about the next generation mobile web. Thank you very much for watching.

source: /Users/lrudl/Dropbox/Rudolf/code/pollux/datasets/raw/alexk/transcripts/transcript-Chat with Alex Komoroske (Live from Google I_O 2015) - Coffee with a Googler.mp4_spaced.txt

messages.content:
SPEAKER_5: I won't get started.

SPEAKER_1: Josh, did you already do your opening calendar thingy?

SPEAKER_5: Nope, starting right now. I am going to leave the calendar off, though, so that we don't have any issues with getting Alex's slides back on.on the screen there. So yeah, thank you, everybody. Welcome back to the Summer of Protocols guest talk series. This series is part of an 18-week program where a cohort of 34 researchers are exploring the various aspects of protocols. You can find out more at summerofprotocols.com. Today, we're talking with Alex Komorowski. Our next session will be tomorrow, which will be our first researcher-hosted guest talk, and that will be with Matthew Lutz on swarm protocols of ants. The pre-meeting reading material will be linked in the calendar at summerprotocols.com. And so, with that, I will hand it off to Venkat to introduce Alex for us today.

SPEAKER_1: All right. Thanks, Josh. About Alex, I think the best way to introduce him is to talk about how I recommend stuff. So, I recommend a lot of books to people and a few essays. It's mostly books, then essays. But I think I've only ever recommended slide decks twice in my life. Once I saw the slide decks of John Boyd, you know, Patterns of Conflict and all those other famous presentations of John Boyd. But the other is actually Alex here. And that's how I first got to know him. He had a really, I don't know, powerful deck called Coordination Headwinds. And it's one of those decks where if you've been an insider in the tech world and you kind of know what it takes to get like large corporate machines actually do what they're supposed to do. So not even like hacking them or, you know, subverting them or like running around them, but just getting them to even do what they're like nominally meant to do. You kind of recognize all the problems that he talks about, but if you haven't been in that environment, it'll be like. What's the slide deck going on and on about weird people problems in large companies? So it's one of the best slide decks ever. And it's the, I think, only slide deck that's ever been the reason I've riffed off on a blog post. So yeah, Alex is a veteran of the tech industry. And as well as that, he also runs, I believe, a community. I forget the name at the moment. It shares links.
The Fox Collective, yeah. I'm a member of the Fox Collective, one of the facilitators of it, yeah.

SPEAKER_1: So that, and also lately, fun little AI projects. And I'm really looking forward to what he's got here, which looks like it brings all his various products and platform whispering talents together in whatever this is. All right, over to you, Alex.

Thanks very much. So really before I get going, I hopefully will have a bunch of time left over for questions at the end. To give you a little bit of context about who I am, one of the things I did for a number of years, I led Chrome's Web Platform PM team. So I was one of the handful of people that decided to fork WebKit to Blink and how to set up that broader community and how to navigate the messy world of web standards. So a lot of this talk comes from that experience and from other experiences I did in augmented reality and Android and a few others as a product manager working in platforms and ecosystems. This is a cut-down version of a deck that I have, a very long deck I have on my site. This is the first time I've ever presented it, I think. So apologies if I get it. I go a little bit fast. It will be relatively abstract, but hopefully it will help give us a nice foundation for an interesting discussion. So one thing I want to clarify before I get going, when I use the word 1P in this context, I mean the platform provider, the entity who is creating or writing. the platform. And 3P, I mean the developers, the end users of the platform who are using the underlying platform. So let's get started. I think people, when they look at platforms, often try to approach it very much like an engineering project, like a high modernism kind of approach. And I think it's way better just to approach it kind of like gardening-a living thing. And that's the underlying thesis of this approach. So first today, we're going to talk about the properties of ideal platforms. Second, we're going to talk about how to evolve a platform in place. And third, we're going to talk about how to grow one from scratch. So let's dive into the characteristics of an ideal platform. So imagine you've got the bedrock, you've got whatever exists today. There's some use case somewhere out in the world that would add some value for some user. And if it were to be built, you would have to do a lot of work to do that. You have to add a lot of code and ship it and build processes, whatever it takes to actually ship the thing. And it might be that there is not enough value to justify reaching all the way up, which means you just end up not addressing that use case. So maybe you go after a smaller use case somewhere else. But that's kind of a shame, right? There's a value, a thing that could be created in the world. And this is just one use case. There's often huge amounts of use cases that could be built by different entities. And imagine that they did this. They all had to rebuild a whole bunch of stuff that is actually pretty common. What if you could lift up the bedrock so you allowed multiple third parties to have less room they had to build to do something useful? And that's the fundamental idea of a platform. You just lift people up so that they have less to reach to unlock interesting use cases. So let's imagine really quickly that this platform is providing some service that developers find useful. And then it gets really greedy. And it goes, hey, you know what? I'm going to start charging you 10 times more than I used to. Well, that's no longer viable for those users to use. And so they might say, well, I can't use the platform anymore. Well, they can't drop all the way down and build it from scratch because their use case might not be viable to build this much versus this much. And that means that the platform does have some amount of power over the underlying users of that platform, especially to the extent that there aren't alternatives. And so it's important for platforms to earn the trust of the users and developers who use them. Now, some use cases, some places will have less of a worst-case scenario. So if it's a fully open source project done entirely in the open with contributors from many different organizations, then, OK, worst-case scenario, you fork if you really need to.versus the worst-case scenario for a proprietary thing that's some API or service running somewhere that has no replacement, well, the worst-case scenario, if that platform cuts you off as a developer, is pretty bad. So the amount of trust you need differs in different contexts. But what's most important is platforms have to be an earned trust of the developers. The next one is it's only possible to build things on top of the horizontal exposure of the platform. So imagine if the platform only went a little bit, not very wide. There's certain use cases that just aren't possible to build unless it builds out underneath them. So imagine you're doing research for which use cases you want to support. You identify a couple of clusters of use cases. You build bits of the platform under them. That's great. That's fine. But almost without question, there's other use cases you didn't really fully weren't aware of between those other use cases. And if you don't build the platform underneath them, they don't activate. So if it's not too much extra trouble, it's a good idea to make your platforms a little bit wide to fill in the gaps between known use cases. And every so often, you do want to extend out just beyond where you think there might be use cases, just in case there's adjacent ones. So that's the second characteristic of platforms that typically are wide. They go broad to allow other use cases to build on top of them. So far, we've been thinking about our platform like a monolith, just some big hunk of black box of code. But in reality, of course, inside, there's a bunch of internals of how it's actually built. There's a difference between the exposed semantics, the things that external developers can see and interact with, and the internal implementation of them. Inside, the implementation, probably like any well-built system, is layered as built of internal layers that build on one another internally. The top layers tend to be very specific or opinionated, but more general than generic. But there's always this kind of inherent layering. Code is not magic. Code ultimately has to be built on top of other code that makes sense. And I call it inductively knowable. At each point, you as a developer could peel back one layer of the code and understand that next layer down. And you could keep on peeling back the layers of the onion all the way down to the bottom if you really need to. So code works best when things are well layered and you minimize the amount of magic. In that, you can explain each layer of the platform, each layer of the implementation in terms of the other things. We use these low-level building blocks. messages.content: For those of you just joining, I'm Alex Komorowski, a product manager in Chrome's open web platform team. And I wanted to show you a few examples of some of the cool stuff you can do on the cutting edge of the web platform. Now, the web platform is already immensely powerful, but it's learning entirely new classes of capabilities. So let's say you want to make a photo booth app. Well, you're out of luck, right, because you can't access the webcam. Wrong. In fact, in true web platform fashion, it requires minimal code. So here I have a video that will autoplay, and I have a minimal script that will connect up the video tag up to the stream. And that gives me this, which is my face live in the browser, my webcam being captured and displayed right in the browser. But of course, it's just a normal video element, so if I wanted to, I could make myself, let's say, rotate in 3D space with the transform. There, my face rotating in 3D space live in the browser. I could also, of course, go for more of a hipster effect and say add a sepia effect, a filter of sepia. And there we go. I'm rotating. With a sepia effect, kind of a hipster vibe going. Of course, it's just a simple example, but with a little bit more effort, you can do some really cool stuff. So, here is a really cool photo booth app that's running in HTML5. It's got a lot of neat effects, but I think my favorite is the kaleidoscope, which is kind of mind-blowing. Okay, so that's some of the cool stuff you can do with webcam. Let's talk about audio. So historically, audio has been kind of hard to do in the web platform. It required you to do things like use Flash. But now there's actually some very powerful audio APIs available on the web platform. So here is an example of the Web Audio API, which is in Chrome. It allows you to connect up a context, create a source, create a filter node, connect the source to the filter. You can do arbitrarily complex graphs and also playback sounds on very specific timing, which is really important for things like games. Now, this basic API allows things like this, which is one of my favorite examples. It's called Plink. It will generate audio live in the browser on client side. So I can make all kinds of cool sounds. But the other important thing here is that these other people who are playing music live here, I have no idea who they are. They're just random people on the internet. We're using real-time communications to send our movements back and forth to each other and playing music dynamically together. Really, really cool stuff. This is another example. It's DJ Breakpoint, we call it. Just a quick, simple example, pulling together a number of these technologies we've talked about. So normally I'd have another friend playing along with this, but I don't have any friends. I guess I don't have any friends online right now, so I'll just have two windows open here side by side. But I've got real-time chat here. You can see that it shows up immediately on both sides. And if I want to play music, it's really simple. I can just drag a music file from my desktop that will upload on both sides. And once it's uploaded, it will start playing on both sides. And we'll see visualization of it going on on both sides. So you can see we've got a really cool visualization here that looks really nice. It's also got a reflection, if you look closely. The other thing to notice is in just a second, my avatars are warming up a little bit here, I think. But in just a second, you'll see them start dancing. There we go. You can see my avatars starting dancing along with the beat. So analyzing the sound, figuring out where the beat is, and bouncing along with it. Some really neat stuff. Okay, so that's audio. What about something harder like 3D? So this is an example that's running live in the browser. It's a very complicated scene with complicated physics. I can make ripples in the water, very complicated lighting effects. This is running directly in the browser with no plugins. It's JavaScript and WebGL. Really amazing stuff here. But let's say you're a game developer, and you've already spent a lot of time to build your app for another platform, and you want to bring it to the web platform. What will we have you covered? Native Client is a technology built directly into Chrome that allows you to take existing code bases and import them to the web platform with no plugins and with a robust security model. So this is Airmech. It's a really cool game. It's in alpha right now. It's using Native Client to run natively in Chrome. It originally came from an existing codebase, was ported to the native client with minimal effort. It's got amazing 3D graphics, you can shoot around, you can turn into a Transformer dude for some reason, not really sure why. But the other cool thing about this is I'm actually controlling it using a wireless game controller, live. So let's take a step back. This is a game with amazing 3D graphics, ported from an existing codebase with minimal effort, and using a wireless controller all-running natively on the web platform without any plugins. That's all the examples I have for today. The web platform is capable of crazy, amazing, mind-blowing stuff. It's evolving incredibly quickly and I just can't wait to see where it goes next. Thanks.

Now, before we get really started, I just want to reiterate that this is an intermediate level talk. So if you saw Eric's talk just before this, before lunch, you're probably all set. If not, there are going to be a lot of points where you're just scratching your head. And what you'll want to do is review some of the other specs and tutorials on some of these emerging web standards afterwards.

And you'll see now I can actually open that node, and the grayed-out document fragment is actually a sign that you're in Shadow DOM now. So this entire little world inside this tag is encapsulated from the outside world. There's a hard border at the edge. Okay. So that's easy because that's Chrome. So what about something harder? All right. In this VM, which I've paused because that seems to be the best way to get Windows to actually stay up for this period of time for the purposes of this presentation, I've got it here in IE as well in Windows 8.

So it's the same code using again these concepts of shadow DOM web components but it's working in IE. So how did we do this? How did we make this happen? Well, the answer is that we're impatient. We're really excited about web components, but we didn't want to wait to use them until they were in every modern browser. The other important thing is we work on the Chrome team with some of the folks who are actively standardizing the web component stuff in the standards bodies. And when you're designing new web standards, it's really important to experiment with them, especially if they're very complex. What you don't want is you work on the specification for a while, you build consensus, you implement it, you ship it, and then you use it and you go, ooh, wow, that's really hard to use. Is this something that you expect we'll need to use only when we can tightly control the environment in which it's running? I came in a little late, so if there's backwards compatibility, that's my question.

Just to reiterate, actually, the whole approach we're taking is building a layer of polyfills that sort of mocks out the future. So Matt's using Canary, so we could actually show you real Shadow DOM being used. But this stuff should work across all modern browsers, i.e., Safari, Firefox, Chrome. And over time, as they implement these standards, that sort of foundation layer will get thinner and thinner.
SPEAKER_3: So when you say modern browsers, I assume there will be a level that we'll need to test for and then?

That's correct. So one of the goals here is to really live like we're in the future. And if you're staring in the rear view mirror, you're headed for trouble. So we want to make sure that we can actually simulate the future in a high fidelity way.

SPEAKER_2: When you pulled up the Xcode bits from the Mozilla team, you said there was I'm sorry? X tags. X tags. You said it was significant because you were pulling that in from a third party, you know, another vendor. Where's the actual technical significance there if they're kind of just doing the same thing? I didn't see, like, a threshold between Polymer and X tags.

SPEAKER_0: So generally, if you wanted two libraries to play together previously, they had to either be built very specifically to do that and not introduce kind of system-level dependencies. Or they would collide and you'd just have to get lucky. So the idea is with these things being encapsulated, it's that you don't have those system-level dependencies and really the world inside of that element, which is what using Mozilla's code is completely separate from the world outside of that element. So you can mix and match these things together a lot easier without having to deal with those collisions.

So the idea, too, before Web Components, you had to make everything in script. And so you have these conventions of what it means to be a component. And everyone's conventions have to align for them to be interoperable. With Web Components, people can align their conventions on Web Components, the standards. And then that makes them interoperable much more easily.
SPEAKER_2: So did you have another library you pulled in to use those X tags and they just coexist then because they're? Creating DOM elements that are all happy together?

So theoretically, you could just have a different include. You could include a component in the future. You could include a component from Polymer's UI library. You could include a component from XTags. You could intermingle the two. Or a third-party framework that's just yet to be created. It's all in that HTML import thing.

SPEAKER_2: So it's that UI aspect that's above Polymer and everything else that was significant in that respect. Yeah. XTag versus your components. Yeah. Gotcha. Thank you.

SPEAKER_7: So I would confirm a simple question. This Polymer tool also works with Chrome Packaged Apps?

SPEAKER_0: Oh, does it work right now? Right now it does not work. The way we did the polyfill violates CSP. And Chrome Apps is very specific about that. In the very near future, once the HTML imports feature, which is the thing that's loading things from across domains, is done natively, which is pretty soon, then it'll work. So actually, HTML imports is being implemented, right?

It's just started being implemented in Chrome. Yeah, so you can still use parts. So as Daniel was pointing out, you can still use some parts, like document .register, even if you can't use some of these other pieces, like HTML imports. We've got one in the back.

SPEAKER_6: Yeah. You were talking about modern browsers. I was wondering if you could elaborate on mobile support.

SPEAKER_0: So mobile, it's Mobile Safari and Chrome for Android. Chrome and Android browser. Chrome for Android. Yeah, Chrome for Android at the moment is the only one we're testing on. OK, thanks. Yeah, it's very early days.

SPEAKER_4: Back to, like, several years ago, there was actually a similar, like, technique that was aimed to achieve the similar level of encapsulation, which is XML plus XSLT. The essence of it is, the picture of that is essentially you will create a heavy element-focused, say, web interface instead of, like, having to take care about all the underlying layers. What would you compare that to Web Components?

I think there's been a number of things that are similar to Web Components in the past. None of them have really stuck around for various reasons. There's the things like XBL, which is still used, actually, in parts of Gecko. You've got things like the HTC stuff from Microsoft. You've got a bunch of other things that aim to solve similar kinds of problems.

SPEAKER_0: Web Components is now like an emerging set of specs that is ‑‒ So the difference here is that you're using the same technologies you already use on the Web today, right? You're using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. There's actually nothing new here. Whereas XSLT, I mean, I did a whole framework in XSLT like 10 years ago, and that was a, you know, to debug that was one of the more insane things I've ever done in my life. Yeah.

SPEAKER_4: Thank you.
So we're actually out of time, I believe. So if you have more questions, you can come find us up here after the talk. Thanks. Thanks.

messages.content: I view platform development as a swarm of energy of uncoordinated entities. Sometimes it's literal developers inside of an organization. You can visualize it as a swarm. Sometimes it's like users of the platform, people on an open source project who are contributors. And then there's the kind of thing that makes it something coherent out of it, the selection pressure that kind of selects into a thing that is more coherent. I think the power of these approaches has tilted way too much into the selection pressure. We are overdue for a swing back. I personally think that generative AI helps significantly with that, allowing a lot of tinkerer energy, a new era of tinkerer energy.

SPEAKER_1: Hello, everybody. Welcome back to the Boundaryless Conversations podcast. On this podcast, we meet with pioneers, thinkers, and doers, and we talk about the future of business models, organizations, markets, and society in this rapidly changing world. Today, I am joined by a new regular co-host, my colleague at Poundless, Shruti Prakash, who is joining from Indonesia. Hello, Shruti. Good to have you. Hi.

SPEAKER_2: Nice to be here as well, Simone. Thank you.

SPEAKER_1: And today, we are joined by kind of a true champion of open platform thinking, Alex Komoroske. Alex, hello. Hi. Alex, thank you so much for joining us today. I think for those that think about platforms, open platforms more specifically, the implications in organizational matter, you are really like an inspiration. And I really encourage the people that didn't familiarize with the content yet to do it as soon as possible, because it's really, really important. I recently met Alex. Because we shared speaking as guest lecturers at the Summer of Protocols, which is a program that the Ethereum Foundation started before the summer to go deep into the implications of the evolution between platforms and protocols, essentially. So the role that protocols play in society, in technology, and so on. And we will have an extensive bio for Alex in the podcast notes, and you find all the links to your decks and other pieces of work that you have been churning out in the last few years. But I think we should definitely peek into your expertise on platform development and maybe just mention that you've been working on things like the Chrome web platform, which has billions of users daily at the moment, and Stripe on strategy. But maybe we can give to our listeners a little bit of a quick one-minute overview of what drives your work and interest and research today.

When it comes to platforms, yeah, and a lot of it grew out of a necessity just when uh for many years working on and leading Chrome's Web Platform PM team, uh of like you got a reason through how to navigate a platform problem I think product managers are often under the misunderstanding that they're in way more control of their product and its usage than they actually are because this notion of like I'm the CEO of the product has this kind of control mentality. And platform PMs have to contend with the fact that there's at least another layer of actors in the middle that they have to influence to then lead to the outcomes in the real world they care about. An open platform PM where you have multiple browser vendors, many of whom, you know, don't always get along, trying to figure out how to do something coherent is like a place where you fundamentally have to come to grips with what is happening in the platform and the power dynamics of it. Later, I also realized how some of the tactics I used to navigate large organizations were basically exactly the same as the strategies I was using, intuitively using to navigate large open ecosystems. And they kind of fit together in my head. And since then, I've gotten to learn quite a bit more about complex adaptive systems and other lenses of power dynamics and others to understand and sort of represent and unearth the intuition that was developed. In the forge of many years of experience in a very challenging environment to understand them and pack them and see where else they might apply.

SPEAKER_1: I think it's good to maybe double-click quickly on this idea of platforms as complex adaptive systems, which is, I think, one of the major elements of the way you look at platforms. And it's very interesting for me because when we started our work at Boundless, or even before Boundless, 10 years ago, when I started to transform design and I was kind of dropping these canvases for helping people to kind of grapple with the complexity of creating a multi-sided system with so many people, so many players in the ecosystem, and so on. There's always some kind of reductionist approach that you have to embrace because otherwise the complexities would be overwhelming, right? But besides the necessary simplifications you have to make to be able to try and enact a design in a platform, can you maybe expand on how do you go from your vision as a platform developer, the things, the ideas that you may have about the ecosystem, the services the ecosystem needs, and so on, and maybe introduce a few key concepts that people can use to really understand your idea of rational platforms and platforms as complex systems. I'm thinking of, for example, the way you introduced the idea of use cases, layers, horizontal and vertical layers. Maybe you can give us a few pointers that can help the listeners to start understanding your point of view on platforms as complex adaptive systems?

I think the simplest way in the core of it, and why I titled that deck the way I did, was it's the gardening mindset. As opposed to the builder mindset. The builder mindset is highly engineered, highly focused on control. There are no externalities to the system. You figure out what to build and then you build it and then it works. In a gardening mindset, you very clearly have some other agents in the thing that you were trying to influence and create, but you fundamentally can't control them directly. You can put up different scaffolding. You can trim back things. You can definitely have an impact on the outcome, but in a fundamental way, you don't directly control it. And that's the mindset shift. I think that's so important. When I talk a lot about some of the emotional feelings of navigating complex problems, which platforms are a perfect type of, but I think it's really a lot of problems that we in our jobs face day to day, is people say, they often, I think, incorrectly say, that sounds like nihilism. You sound like you were saying, 'Give up.'and don't do anything and nothing you do matters. And I think that's fundamentally wrong. I think that this is actually quite an empowered stance. I think it is by acknowledging your own limitations of the actual space of actions that you are permitted to make based on the constraints on you, you actually realize how much power you do have to choose among those decisions in a coherent way that leads to large coherent outcomes. And so people, I think, often want to have control in the small. And so they, which is an illusion, I think to some degree. And so they end up giving up on the ability to influence the large arcs of your system. And so if you have, one way of looking at this is when you have uncoordinated entities, lots of different agents, lots of different developers or people or coworkers or whatever, by default, it's just Brownian motion. Everybody's just going in a direction and the coherence at the whole, there is no coherence. It's just an overall like entropic kind of expansion. And if you, however, if you can get even the teensiest amount of bias of like people are 10% more likely to go in that direction, overall, the coherence of the overall behavior of the swarm goes up significantly, right? Just a little bit of an edge, a consistent edge, and it pulls everybody in that direction. And this is one of the reasons that North Stars can be very powerful clarifying concepts within an organization, within an ecosystem, within a platform, having a very clear, a plausible and inspiring North Star, that other people, that everybody knows about and everybody shares. And that can lead to very coherent outcomes. And creating such a North Star is quite difficult. They're often very low resolution. They must be because you can't control the details of it. You can only control the grand sweep of the thing or give more of the path that it might take on the grand sweep of things. And I think that's very confusing to people because it's very, everyone's used to control in the small as opposed to like this kind of like sweeping arc. In the bigger picture.

SPEAKER_1: You mentioned a very important word that we use a lot in our work, which is coherence. Is coherence really an attribute that we should be seeking in modern platform development? And especially, I want to make this question in relation to the idea of what we normally call open platforms versus closer platforms, let's say you can think of maybe aggregators or or you know, or platforms which are more like more end-user oriented than the more open which are typically more like enabling platforms. So is coherence important and especially when we think about building an open versus more like closer use case-oriented plan?

I think you do need coherence to some degree because otherwise, you get background noise, which is diffuse. It's entropy. And so you need something that gives a bias so that it accumulates into something more than background noise. And that's so that requires some kind of coherence of like multiple agents pointing in roughly ish the same direction, at least some pockets. So I would argue that it's way easier to find. Coordinating and getting 18 or so different actors to all agree on a direction from a standstill is basically impossible. It's really, really hard. However, if you see that a couple of them are already pointing in roughly the right direction and they could say, hey, we should like work together. It's like, oh, okay. Like that becomes easy. And, you know, you can tweak and go instead of going default divergent, you go like this. So now you're default convergent. Okay. And so now this gets momentum. And now other people nearby who may be at the beginning, I'm not really 100% sure that maybe they were oriented like this. As this gets momentum, it pulls people's incentives in just a little bit. And so now there's people that before were on the margin in a way. Now they're on the margin in, they come in. This creates the pull even stronger. And over time, this gravity wall dynamic can pull in more people once it gets a bunch of momentum. And people go, their opinion of what they want to do individually is overwhelmed by whatever. I'll just go and do the thing that everybody else is doing because it's better to do something with momentum. So you can get this coherence. But it's quite hard to do from a standstill. And I do think that you do need local pockets. If everyone's doing their own individual thing that never adds up to anything more, you don't get anything. You just get noise. And so there's a section at the end of I have this essay about shelling points and organizations and about, I think it's a, it's a treatment of like game-theoretic shelling points. And at the end, the very last section of that essay, almost gets poetic. And someone called me out. I was like, are you writing poetry? What are you trying to do here? But like talking about the importance of these little, there's all this background noise happening, all this stuff that's just random. And every so often, a little shelling point of a thing, a little seed of an idea that is coherent.pops out that can then start accumulating energy from nearby things. And it can sometimes grow into something very large. But I think you do need those shelling points, those little pockets, those little seeds of coherence. Otherwise, you don't get a thing collectively. And that's really the challenge in open platforms. How do you design for the layers that have coherence at the protocol layer to allow incoherence above? So like the scaffolding for the incoherence and the exploratory energy. But you need something that coordinates them.pulls people in and makes them say 'default'to going from that sort of, again, that default divergent to default convergent on that level.

SPEAKER_1: When you were talking about this, I was kind of reconnecting this conversation. When you speak about this, I feel like your use of the concept of a North Star, for example, and this idea of things coalescing around these kinds of elements. It's very important, but what I'm seeing, for example, now, when I look at the Web3 narrative, it's more like an idea that everybody's going to build a little piece and then you are free to compose everything together. So there is this lack of, it's like there is a rebound from the centralized platforms of the early 2000s. And everybody felt like, you know, you're too controlling, you're too dictatorial. So we want to develop a new ecosystem which is made of small pieces, scarcely related together. We just build our own small piece and then everybody will compose, you know, and we'll take Lego bricks and make something more complex. So how do you feel about this kind of shift of narrative from more kind of coherent platform visions into a universe of small projects that develop small pieces and then somebody will bundle them together?

I think it's great. I view platform development as a swarm of energy of uncoordinated entities. Sometimes it's literal developers inside of an organization. You can visualize it as a swarm. Sometimes it's like users of the platform, people on an open source project who are contributors. And then there's the kind of thing that makes it something coherent out of it, the selection pressure that kind of selects into a thing that is more coherent. I think the power of these approaches has tilted way too much into the selection pressure and we are overdue for a swing back. I personally think that generative AI helps significantly with that, allowing a lot of tinkerer energy, a new era of tinkerer energy and empowered things. One of my favorite essays is a very old essay from Clay Shirky in 2004 about situated software. And I think this is a very powerful idea whose time has come because of the generative AI. That's a whole other trend that we can go down. But I do think that I 100% agree. I would point out that what happens in a lot of those things, the swarming behavior, is you have to have some kind of coherent API, which is a boundary between systems that people know is roughly constant. And not necessarily it never changes, but something that you can rely on as a thing that these things will plug in together. So like Unix, everything's a file. Great boundary. It just fits in really, really nicely. And everything can plug together like Lego bricks. Gordon Brander, who's, I don't know if he gave a talk at the Summer Protocols, but he's doing subconscious and he's a good friend. And I would think a literal genius. And he talks quite a bit about the power of these clarifying protocols for things to fit together. Protocols, by the way, don't have to emerge on purpose. Sometimes they emerge because like somebody, everyone just starts using some protocol of some open source system, like the boundary of that system. People start going, 'nah, I'll use that.'And then over time, more people glom onto it. It becomes a formal boundary that people now rely on. And now when someone goes to like, like the owner of that project goes to like tweak that boundary, people go, 'Whoa, we're all relying on that thing.'So it can absolutely happen kind of unexpectedly of people voting with their feet kind of, and choosing what code to build on, what things they think have value to build on top of. But so protocols can emerge. Unintentionally. And you get some real weird things, though, when you get things that weren't designed to be, weren't conceptually wanting to be federated protocols. With federated protocols, you have to assume all your agents are going to move very, very slowly. And so that means you want the basest semantics, really small, sparse semantics. You don't want a lot of sugar on top because these are extra semantics you have to maintain and change over time. And those things can be intentional.

SPEAKER_2: Take from that cue, Alex. So, for example, how you speak about maybe semantics being a conscious maybe choice when you start off. I want to understand how maybe in complex ecosystems like this, where, like you said, it's chaotic, even though it might be, let's say, expansive in nature, but it's essentially chaotic, right? So how does all of that translate into an organization? How do you, let's say, see the connection between the two and let that be like a corporate or like a startup either way, right? How do you see that translating or impacting our organizational functions today?

I think to some degree, an organization is an assemblage of people that agree to subvert their individual goals to a collective set of goals in that context. One of my favorite little anecdotes is back in the early, before midway through the 19th century United States, the United States was a plural noun. The United States are. A great place to live, and then after so some time after the Civil War it became a singular noun: The United States is. And that emphasizes the assemblage of them versus the individual 'swarm'of it, and there's something very different about the way you think-which one matters more, the collection or the individuals in that context? And which one has more agency, which one has the the power to, the rightful power to the side? That's to some degree, the agents have to decide that they want to participate in that thing. And so you see it coming together where people say, 'Oh, our goals are similar and we should work together so that we don't have to rebuild a bunch of things or we'll get more momentum than we would get individually.'But then you might find that you have a disagreement and you're like, 'See ya, bye; I'm going to fork and go over in this other direction.'So when you have something very, when it's very informal. It's very easy to break up. In the same way, when you're just dating someone informally, very easy to break up. When you're married, way harder to break up. And so you're more committed. And one of the things that you've done when you get married is you've made commitments to demonstrate, here's how seriously I feel about this relationship. I'm going to make it really hard for myself to leave, to show you how committed I am to this relationship. And you get some of the same dynamics that happen in organizations. Okay, you sign a contract with a company that you are currently an employee of, and it has certain very clear transfers of agency in certain contexts. So I think that it shows up wherever people want to think that it would be more useful to work together on something. Coordination, when I did my original slime mold deck, Venkat did a really nice piece that was very flattering about coordination headwinds and how they rule everything around us. They're everywhere. People think that, I know I use this lens a lot, but like, Everyone wants and expects hard things to be hard for interesting reasons. And the reality is the vast majority of hard things are hard for totally boring reasons. And it's just the coordination challenge of getting lots of individual agents to point in roughly the same direction for some period of time is really, really, really, really, really hard. And it's just the amount of energy in society that goes into this is huge. And I think to some degree, I mean, technologies like AI and the internet changed this cost to some degree on the margin, but on a fundamental level, I think this is not even just like a huge, like, man, humanity is kind of silly that we spend all our time coordinating. No, I think it's a fundamental thing. I think it's like, it's a thing that's expected to show up in any complex adaptive system fundamentally. And over again, we're increasing the information transfer rate, which allows larger coordination structures to be plausible. But I don't think it's, I think it's a fundamental characteristic.

SPEAKER_1: Of like a fundamental, first principles it emerges into any any plausible universe must have this dynamic kind of thing-is there even the slightest chance if I understand well that uh what you are um I don't want to say arguing for but you want to suggest maybe uh is that when you deal with complex ecosystems and complex platforms a couple of useful things to do are agreeing on the North Star, one side, so the big vision maybe, and agreeing on a small set of kind of commitments that could be, for example, interfaces, right? That we all agree we can discuss maybe once for all and then kind of stick to this promise. As we develop our own piece of the puzzle. I think so.

But I also see people do is like, okay, I want to coordinate with all 18 of these different people. Step one, get them all to agree on a thing. That's impossible. That's a miracle. Stop right there. Like that will not work. So instead, what I do is I say, listen, I'm going to sketch out where I'm going and why I think it's interesting and why other people I think might find it interesting too. And I'm not going to try to convince you. I'm just going to do it. I have, this doesn't require me to convince you to do action on this. I'm going to start tinkering on these very short-term things that start accumulating value in that direction. And if you think it's worthwhile, feel free to pitch in. That's great. And you can help even tweak the vision. That's great too. But you come to me if you want to participate. Because when you find someone who has a self-selecting interest into your thing, they are way more motivated as opposed to trying to force someone. It's like trying to force someone to be in love with you. It doesn't work. It doesn't work that way. Like you can allow, it can be more likely that someone falls in love with you by being a caring, thoughtful person.um knowing that that letting them know that you would uh welcome their you know their attention and interest but like don't try to force somebody to and so once you get momentum if you find a set of actions that will get you some momentum um even small amounts then you will naturally you might find that people go oh that's kind of cool i want to be part of that and then that gets more momentum and then it builds and now before you know it you actually have quite a large thing and if you didn't it's okay like this is where i have an essay about the doorbell in the jungle this notion of people do strategies and they do this big grand thing. And they say, well, we shouldn't even do any of this unless this big grand thing is impossible. And they go for the hardest part first. Say, first, we're going to do this big, bold bet. My God, no. Sketch out a thing that is coherent, that people go, ah, I can see how that could work. And then, again, a North Star that is plausible and inspiring. And this should be like two pages. It should not be long. It should be, ah, here's why this thing could work. And people go, ah, I guess, yeah, sure, okay. And then figure out what are the small amount of actions that you could take right now that would almost, but would very likely pay for themselves, which is to say very low cost or very clear value that's unlocked. Like very clear, like I've got eight customers who are like banging on the door asking for exactly this thing and telling me to take their money. Like neat, if I invest three hours of effort to build that feature, I will get money for it. So like, okay, what's the likelihood I regret doing that work? Very low, because worst case scenario, it paid for itself. And then what you do is you just look for more signals of demand. And if at any point you stop seeing demand, just pause, just stop developing it. That's fine. But when you start seeing demand again, start investing again. And if you have multiple of these, eight or so of these in different directions, the likelihood that any one of them is firing in demand at any given time is pretty high. And so this looks again to people like nihilism. They say, oh, you're saying you don't have a strategy. No, I'm saying I have a really good strategy. It is a meta-strategy that works quite well and is likely to work. No matter what is happening in the rest of this world. But people so often just go, 'I'm going to', step one, convince all 18 people that this is a good idea. Like, that's not going to work.

SPEAKER_1: That's extremely interesting because we are arguing as well about boundaries for organizations to embrace a different organizational structure, which is a little less, well, actually not a little, but a lot less bureaucratic than usual. So when you say, for example, you shouldn't have a strategy in mind. You should kind of let the strategy unfold itself. I think it's really resonating with what we do, for example, with the TRIO model, that is our organizational framework that we have developed following the example of Haier's Rendon Hay and other models. I recall that when we interviewed, for example, the CEO of GE Appliances, which is a US-based company that just, well, actually just, you know, a couple of years ago, adopted the RenderName model, which is based on micro-entrepreneurial units generating their own P &L, essentially. You can think of something like that. He said, we don't have a strategy. The units have strategies, which resonates a lot with what you're saying. But, you know, should it start from the question of how do you organizationally deal with this? We are used to organizations being much more directive in terms of strategy, in terms of management. I can think of, for example, the recent turn of kind of narrative at Airbnb. We've been listening to Brian Chesky in the last six months saying we stopped doing agile, independent development. We just centralized all releases in twice a year, release trains and so on. How can the management in organizations be comfortable with building products and platforms in this way, with so little control on the outcomes?

But my answer sounds so pat and so silly. It's trust. Trust. Trust makes it all work. And especially when you've hired very good people, it's going to be like, you have to, when you love it, let it go. Kind of thing you know, so you got to give like people will know and do things that you don't fully understand and they won't be able to represent to you why they are a good idea and sometimes it's like literally information they cannot share with you um because of the power dynamics and and you know what have you or it wouldn't make sense you know or take or take forever to explain and um so it's just a lot of like hire good people, give them quite a bit of leeway, and help ensure that they can think long term. You can think of trust as the lubricant of um of an organization. And it's the thing that allows the organization to navigate ambiguity and to get stronger from it, not weaker. And by default, ambiguity destroys an organization in a low-trust environment. And every little bit of thing is like, oh, I think that person is just trying to be lazy or like do this thing for their own personal gain. Like, great. I mean, you're screwed at that point. Like there's nothing. You're now able to descend into this finger-pointing kind of thing. A lens I think is interesting for organizations, by the way, one of my favorite little lenses, and we use this a fair bit on Flux, is Kayfabe. And kayfabe is this notion from professional wrestling. And what it means is a thing that everybody knows is fake, that everybody acts like is real. And I think kayfabe is a useful lens for understanding what actually happens in organizations. And organizations, kayfabe is also a little bit of a lubricant in organizations, in the same way that politeness is actually, a lot of politeness is a lot of crushed up little white lies, kind of, but they lubricate interactions in society. So like a little bit of kayfabe is good. It helps make sure everybody is like, yeah, we're going to be bold or whatever. What can happen in organizations in general, and this happens, I think, inexorably to some degree in all organizations over time, is the kayfabe starts off grounded, just a little bit off the ground truth. But then you add more layers and then you add more pressure for big, bold bets. And that means it's harder for people to raise disconfirming evidence up the chain to their manager and say, I don't think that's possible. And so you now have like, they're just playing the game. So now you have something that's decoherent. At a certain point, the kayfabe delaminates from underlying reality and becomes free floating. And this is a very dangerous situation because you now have a thing that looks like it's going in a coherent direction. It is not. The other thing that is very dangerous about this is you as a participant in an organization have to make a decision. messages.content: So I'm Alex Komorowski. I'm Max Heinrichs. We're two product managers on the Chrome's Open Web Platform team. Now, most of this session is going to be just questions and answers from you guys. You'll see there's that Google Moderator link up there. You can leave questions on there, or you can ask them live, whichever you prefer. But that's what we'll spend most of our time on. Before that, Max and I are going to talk for a couple of minutes about Blink and the progress we've made in the past month. So it's kind of crazy, but it's only been a little bit over a month since we announced Blink. And Blink, for those of you who don't know, is a new rendering engine originally based on WebKit that we're using in Chromium. The mission statement of Blink is to improve the open web through technical innovation and good citizenship. So I'm going to talk about the technical innovation part, and Max will talk briefly about the good citizenship part. When we made the decision to create Blink, it was a really hard one that we had to come to terms with. But it came down to fundamentally that Chromium used a different multiprocess architecture from other WebKit-based browsers. And that increased complexity not just for the Chromium team, but for everybody who was using WebKit. So we mentioned back when we made the announcement that we anticipated we could remove about 4.5 million lines from the code base. And that was things like build systems for other ports, JavaScript core, because we use V8, some of the WebKit 2 porting layer, and a number of other things. But the progress we've made since then has really blown our minds. In fact, we've removed now, I think, 8.8 million lines of code. So that's just a really cool stat, I think, that shows that we've been able to streamline this even more than we expected. That's 63% of the repo, I think, and it's also 191 megabytes of code. So less code is better for developers because it means that people who are working on Blink, they can check out the code base faster. They can build faster. And of course, less code is less surface area for bugs. And it hasn't just actually been us that's seen this kind of been able to clean up like this. The WebKit project as well has really been able to clean out some stuff that was really just there for Chromium. So it's been really great for both projects. These are just really easy stats to pull. What actually really matters is developer productivity and how much stuff we can do to improve the stability and performance of Blink. But what I'm most excited about, actually, is not just the performance or the developer productivity, but some of the cool new experiments that we've been able to start that we couldn't have done before. So one of those is a project called Oilpan. And this is just, again, an experiment into seeing if we can move the DOM nodes into a garbage-collected heap. So today, the DOM nodes, they use a reference counting system, and it interacts in a very complex way with the JavaScript engine. And so if this works, it could really help make that much simpler and much easier to understand and less error-prone. Another cool one is called lazy block layout. Again, a very early experiment. The idea is, what if we could make very large but simple layouts much, much faster to do? So if you're only using a handful of features, why can't we make that much, much faster? And if it works, this would allow things like infinite scrolling lists to be much easier to accomplish on the web platform. So those are literally just two totally random examples of stuff that experiments that we started in the past month or so with Blink that we're really excited about. There's a whole bunch of other ones. All the stuff about these and the other ones are on our mailing list.

SPEAKER_12: Thanks, Alex. So, Alex talked a lot about how Blink enables technical innovation. But it's important to balance that momentum with interoperability. We want the whole web to evolve, not just Chrome. That's where the second half of our mission statement comes in. Good citizenship. I'm going to talk about a couple of our initiatives in this area and how they might be relevant to you guys as web developers. The first thing I want to point out is that all of our code is open source, so you can go to our project page, chromium.org slash blink, to download that. I'll show a link at the end. The next thing I want to talk about is our transparent launch review process. Launch review process is healthy for projects in many different ways. For us, it's important for external contributors to understand how they can work with our team to land their code in the code base. It's also important to build consensus early on in the development process and avoid conflicts later. And finally, and this one is really important for us, we don't want to ship proprietary features. And the launch process helps avoid doing that. Basically, when we ship something in Chrome on the web platform, we want all browsers to ship it, not just Chrome. So the way this works in practice is, say you're a Blink engineer and you want to add a web platform feature or somehow change the behavior we expose to web developers. Before you start writing code, we ask you to first send an intent to implement email to our public mailing list, BlinkDev. This email follows a template format. We ask you to provide a link to the specification, a description of use cases and motivation, and finally, give an overview of compatibility risk. And in the last section, what we're looking for is, do other browser vendors think this feature's a good idea? Is it making progress through the standardization process? And usually there's some discussion that follows about whether this is something that belongs in Blink or not. Later, when the feature's ready to ship, we ask them to send another email. And intent to ship email, which is scrutinized a little bit more carefully. It's only been about a month or so, but this launch process seems to be working pretty well. We've had 40 of these intent threads. And of those, we're proud to say that a third have come from non-Googlers. We've had intent threads come from people like Intel, Adobe, Opera, and earlier this week, Microsoft. So pretty exciting stuff. One thing I want to point out before we move on is that this process isn't designed to prevent us from shipping things only in Chrome. It's designed to force us to be very deliberate and thoughtful if we ever decide to do that. So that's just something to keep in mind. Another thing I want to point out is that everything I just talked about is public. You can participate and follow along in our public mailing list, blinkdev at chromium.org. But at the same time, I understand that not everyone has the time to follow all of the intricacies of browser development. And so there are a couple other ways that you can stay up to date on what we're working on that don't involve so much traffic. So I'll talk about them and order them on this slide from most traffic at the top, which is BlinkDev, to the ones with less noise at the bottom. So in addition to BlinkDev, we have a cool site called chromestatus.com, which I'll show you guys now. Basically, this is a spreadsheet. Ooh, the resolution isn't very good right now. Basically, this is a spreadsheet where each row corresponds to a feature that we're currently working on or recently shifted. There's some logistical information like the feature's name, who the owner is, the bugs, its implementation status in Chromium. And then over here, the cells are colored. And these columns correspond to things that we think reflect web citizenship. So for example, progress through standardization, the views of other browser vendors, the excitement from web developers. And our goal with this is to have a nice visual representation of how each feature reflects our web citizenship. And then as you scroll down, you can kind of see the project's overall web citizenship just based on these colors. So right now, this is just an iframed Google spreadsheet, but we're hoping to make progress here. I'll point out how you can actually subscribe to get email notifications when there's changes made. Just go in here and enable notification rules, and you can get emails to stay up to date. I also want to point out that we've gotten great community help with this spreadsheet. Is Masataka here from Japan? Yeah, Masataka has been an enormous help. And we actually haven't met in person yet, so it would be great if you could come up after the talk. But yeah, it's really awesome to see people coming together to help build this set of information. OK, back to the slides. So those are two ways you can stay up to date on features. A third way is through our Developer Relations team. They have Google Plus and Twitter channels that you can follow. And then finally, every time we launch a new Chrome beta, we publish a Chromium blog post which describes all the web platform changes. And that includes things like deprecations, which might be relevant to you guys. So these are a couple ways that you can stay up to date on what's going on in Blink. Again, you can get this information on our project page. So the last thing I want to talk about before we turn it over to Q&A is web platform feature adoption. Google's in a pretty unique place in terms of understanding how people are using the web. We want to make this information available to other people who might be interested in it. So what you're looking at here is data from Google Search Index. Basically, what we did is we ran a graph over all the pages that we have and tried to look for new HTML element tags. And so this top line, for example, is header. And you can see that usage has increased from something like 7% of web pages a couple months ago to near 9% now. So this graph is just kind of useful information, but we're trying to get the similar data for CSS and for JavaScript APIs. And this could be useful in a couple ways that I'll just list out now. One is, say you're a browser engineer and you're trying to optimize CSS. It would be pretty useful to have a list of all of the CSS properties and their frequency across the web so that you can know which code paths would be most efficient to optimize. Another example is, say you're a specification editor and you want to know if you can make breaking changes to the spec without breaking the web. This would help you feel comfortable doing that. For web developers, it's just interesting to see what's reaching the mainstream and what they can use in their websites. And finally, this is important for us as the Chrome, as the Blink team, is that we can get information on when we can responsibly deprecate features. And so a good example of this is with prefixed versus unprefixed features. Oftentimes, when we release an unprefixed version of a feature, we can't remove support for the prefixed version because the web would break. And so we're stuck with this old code path for the prefix version when we really don't need it anymore because we have the unprefixed version. And so we can use this system to track the not only adoption of new features, but also decline of old features, and no one can feel comfortable removing them. So this is the project page I was referencing, chromium.org/ slash Blink. Go there. There's a bunch of information. And then just to summarize real quick, Alex talked a lot about how Blink enables technical innovation on the web. And I talked about how it's important to stay good citizens and make sure that that innovation goes both ways, both from the community to Blink and from Blink to the community. So now we have time for some Q&A, and I'm going to write some people from the team up.
So Darren, Eric, and Paul, you want to talk?

And that allows you to play with all these great goodies. The problem is that prefixing was supposed to be a warning to developers of, don't rely on this. It might go away at any time.
