messages.content: LLMs are really good at writing crappy software on demand for basically free. So, like, it's not good software often, unless you have the right structures and approaches and the right kind of scaffolding to make sure that it's doing the thing that you want it, like what you all are building. And that these radically change how we think of where you would apply software and how you would apply it.

SPEAKER_3: You're listening to the AI Native Dev, brought to you by Tesla.

SPEAKER_1: Hello, Simon Maple here, and welcome to another episode of the AI Native Dev. We have a special one for you this time around as we reshare an AI Native DevCon session, the one where Dion has a chat with Alex Komoroski about the AI developer ecosystem. Now, one of the reasons why we want to share this session is because we actually have a new AI Native DevCon event fast approaching on May 13th, 2025. It's our spring edition, so yeah, we'll be all set having a full edition later in November 2025. At the time of recording, the CFP, the call for papers, and the registration are both open. So head to ainativedev.io forward slash events, where you can submit your proposals to speak at the event, as well as, of course, snag your ticket and your spot for the event as well. It's free to join us. So with that, be sure to subscribe and like the video if you're watching it on YouTube, and I'll hand over to Dion and Alex.

SPEAKER_2: Hey, how's it going, Alex?

SPEAKER_1: Good. How are you?
Thanks for having me.
SPEAKER_2: Yeah, pretty good. Thanks so much for joining us. You know, a little early your time. Whereabouts are you?
I am in Berkeley.
SPEAKER_2: Oh, very nice. That's good. That's good. So yeah, how did Bits and Bobs come about, actually? Was I right in that?

Yeah, so I have the Compendium, which is that sort of custom web app I built over the last number of years to organize my thoughts. And if you look at the Compendium live, you'll think that it's dead. I haven't added anything to it for years. I actually have it open every day, and I add hundreds of notes to it every day in my private working notes area. I use embeddings to help me find commonalities across different ideas. And then each week, I go and spend a few hours on the weekends when the kids are napping, and I distill out. The notes from that week that resonate with me and I developed them a little bit; and it started off actually as a believe it or not, as a practice-a very early version of it was when I used to report to Clay before who recently founded Stripe with Brett Taylor. I would send an email on Monday to him saying: 'Here's the people I'm meeting with', 'Here's the things that I'm doing', and 'Here's a few takeaways, like interesting ideas I think you might find relevant.'And then I continued that practice at Stripe; and then I kind of, that section got deeper and deeper, longer and longer. I started sharing it with more and more people in my internal little strategy study groups, and then into internal blogs. And then I started publishing externally. I would do all that reflection on my own personal time. And yeah, now it's a document that has a stupid number of pages in it; and the it's really slow. One of the things that happens is people, when they're searching in it, it takes so long to load that they're searching and it will Google Docs will think that they're typing into the document. And so it suggests a random edit to the document because they're actually just searching for something in the document. So that happens like once a day. I think I'm probably gonna split it's; I think 700 pages or something. And I, again, I add like 20 pages or so a week, which is just silly.

SPEAKER_2: I know it's probably one of their stress tests. So we're going to get into talking about the future, but curious actually to start with the past a little bit, like with this round of AI and kind of gen AI, when did this first hit home for you? Were you super skeptical at first? And was there a bit of an 'aha'moment that got you thinking like, maybe there's something real here?

Yeah, I've been following, obviously, all the papers and interesting developments at Google Research and others for many years and watching it slowly plot along and some of the silly things that people were doing with the earlier version of chatbots. And the moment it really hit home for me, though, is I have a few groups of people I facilitate of interesting thinkers I just like talking to and having collaborative debate with. And once a year or so, we would get an Airbnb in the woods and we would go and we just have a few days.12 people just for an interesting long-term discussion about what's going to happen in the next decade, not just for crypto but for all of society, and then the next year we did generative AI, and that was I think a few weeks before ChatGPT came out. And we had a number of folks, good friends, people who you know as well, in this session it's just like three days of just thinking and pulling on threads, and afterwards I would sit down and just write 60 pages. So here's what I took away from that discussion and I remember writing down and being like, 'Holy cow, this is going to change everything.'And it's on the cusp of it, it's so many things changing that people haven't realized yet. And then a few weeks later ChatGPT comes out, and it was like just like you know starting bell for everybody, and I knew at that point that like this was going to be like, LLMs are not some flash in the pan. They're not some random technology. Like they are a fundamental disruptive innovation. And I think you need to act like it. I think a lot of people are acting like AI as a sustaining innovation, and I don't think that's true at all. I think it radically changes a number of things that we haven't even, we're like Wile E. Coyote out over the cliff as an industry. We're like, oh, we're just going to slap some AI on this. That's not how that works at all. It actually changes fundamentally. The way that we write software, the way that we can, we can experience things, and it changes the cost structures of everything. It's a really interesting and fascinating thing, but there was that, yeah, it was that period right before ChatDVD came out that I was like, oh, wow.
SPEAKER_2: Got it. That's awesome. So this event is called AI Native Dev, and it can be a little bit of a double entendre, can mean different things. I'm curious what that means for you.

I think. People are currently attempting, they're just jamming AI on the sides of the way that they built software before and the way that they would approach problems. And I think when you embrace LLMs, you write software differently and you think of it, it's almost like, how do you pull the software out of the mind of this LLM? And you do it in a way that's not traditional collaborating with another person. You do it a lot more like what Tesla's doing, I think, of like specs and designing a thing. Like I had a silly little example. There's this game called Sky Joe. It's a fun little card game and we were playing it and I got really into it. I realized, oh, if you could count the cards going through, you would have a better, you could strategically be making better calls. And so I wanted to make a little web app to make it so you could just keep track of the cards and had been. Play out of the deck and it would tell you the probabilities of different cards coming up or whatever this is the kind of thing that would have taken me a few hours on the weekend sit down create a new project from scratch and like slowly add in stuff and what I did instead was I built it in 10 minutes and I just used M-topic artifacts and I did exactly the way I would have done it, I said first write a little function that does this, okay now put that function in this now add TypeScript typing that allows like now like I just told it what I would have done for each of the individual steps. And in 10 minutes, you have this really nice, nicely styled thing that has undo and save and all these little features. And I built it like not by writing any code, but by just coaxing it in the right order with the right steps and things I wanted to do. And that was, that felt like a radically different way of building software to me than sitting down and writing this little project that would have taken me a few hours to do.

SPEAKER_2: Yeah. Do you think that that kind of app, like this notion of a, I think some people are like micro apps and the like is going to change the way that a bunch of even like users and non-developers maybe even getting into this world where they can scratch their own itches where like at the moment, maybe they just like, I could do spreadsheets. So I try and use a spreadsheet to do everything that I can. Like they have these different tools, but they don't feel like they can create custom software.

I think it will, but for a couple of reasons. So I was, a friend of mine is not particularly technical and they knew I was super into playing around with AI. They said, 'I want to see what it can do. I want to have it program.'And so we went to ChatGPT and they were saying a thing they wanted to do, and then they'd give it the HTML and I would copy and paste it into a thing and then load it into separate tab. And that step mystified him. I don't understand what's happening. And the feedback loop just took enough time to like copy-paste. And then sometimes it would tell you you had to surgically put it back in, because it would only update a small portion of it. And then we switched to Anthropic that has artifacts. And that was basically the same loop just in a live preview. And that radically changed it. Instead of it feeling of me handholding him, he just sat there and was doing little things and experimenting with it. And I left, I came back 20 minutes later and he built a relatively complex little toy app. And he was like, I guess I'm a programmer now. I was like, yeah, sure. But it felt like this immediacy, I think felt really nice. I think by default, what we'll see with AI and the current distribution physics is we'll see a proliferation of both hyper-centralized, hyper-aggregators and profusion of lots of little micro apps that are like little toys that they don't really do that much on their own. I think the real trick will be how can you get figure out a system that allows these micro apps to add up to something much larger than some other parts to interact with other micro apps and other experiences and have this emergent possibility. That's quite challenging. I think that requires a fundamentally different kind of like substrate to be built on. And that's one of the things that we're working on. So much of the software, so much of the industry is predicated on the idea that software is expensive to write and cheap to run. And LLMs mess with both of these assumptions because code that uses LLMs to execute, which is not all code in the future, but a fair bit of it, that now has a marginal cost that is above zero, which means it is expensive in some significant way that we're not used to. And secondarily, LLMs are really good at writing crappy software on demand for basically free. So it's not good software often, unless you have the right structures and approaches and the right kind of scaffolding to make sure that it's doing the thing that you wanted, like what you all were building. But it is a, and these radically change how we think of where you would apply software and how you would apply it. Clay Shirky has this old essay from, I think it's 2007, called Situated Software. And it's now off the internet, yet Warren has a mirror of it. It's a great essay, and it talks about situated software as software that is situated to a hyper-specific context. So anytime you've written a spreadsheet formula that's situated software, it's situated specifically in the context of this particular spreadsheet. And anytime that someone looks at someone else's bit of situated software, they go, 'That's a piece of crap. It barely works. It's insecure. It's ugly.'But to the person who built it, it's perfect. It does exactly and precisely what they need. And sometimes in those contexts, actually, what happens if that breaks? You say, I don't know, I'll just. Slap some more situated software on the side. And especially when LLM can help you write the situated software, you can get software that's just right. It's just the right size for you, as opposed to this one-size-fits-none software that we have today in our modern era.

SPEAKER_2: Yeah, that makes sense. Some people I see snarky about, I can just do these little toy things, right? Stay in your zone, stay in the micro apps era. I'm an amazing expert developer and you're never going to be able to touch me.the domain that I understand, my enterprise software and the like. How do you think over time, especially like LLMs are actually going to be able to help, you know, in very broad ways across the spectrum of development that's out there? Or do you think it will never?

Oh, no, it will for sure. And I think folks like Tessel and others are really pushing the limits of what you can get. I think the big story, by the way, the last year, people talk about the quality improvements. To me, it's the context like. Improvements that just radically change the kinds of questions you could ask if you wanted to ask about a large book, what are the major themes of this book? You can't answer it with RagIt's not possible because it's not like there's a 'this is the theme'and that's in the embedding like you have to have a broad context and the more that you have the broad context the more that you can see these subtle patterns keep track of and understand where things are going and so I think we're just beginning stages of figuring out how to get LMS to build large bits of software. I also think one of the things that's exciting about Tesla's approach is Large Language Models (LLMs), one of their superpowers is they're really good at translation. And you can translate English to code. And so, before, like the spec is not just some little documentation. It actually is almost the primary semantic thing. And the actual code is, I think, like compiling the spec into code. And it's unlike a normal compiler where everyone who's learned C at the beginning, at a certain point says the compiler is broken. It's weird to me. This compiler is not broken for this case. So you learn that the compiler is almost always trustworthy. With LLMs, like it can get confused. And the thing that gets confused on most is when it's not high-level locality of the code, when it's in multiple files, it has to coordinate based on a specific phrase or word that has to be consistent in these multiple places, LLMs tend to kind of get a little bit confused by that kind of situation, but there's ways of adding scaffolding and idioms to make it so that they are more likely to stay to a pretty good answer.

SPEAKER_2: Yeah, I think one of my realizations around this was feeling like I was using English to poke at things just like you were talking about, but then all of that information was lost. It was like in the chat session. And it was gone and it was like, but that was like my actual requirement.
Like, yeah, it was like recording the steps of things to get to that result. Yeah.
SPEAKER_2: So it felt like I'm compiling this thing and then just taking my compiled output with me.
Yeah. Wait a minute.

SPEAKER_2: Isn't the other thing, the important thing to stay around? So what if that could be the canonical piece instead? So that's why specs made a lot of sense to me. As you've poked around with all these things, what have you found? You've mentioned some of these, like that LLMs are particularly good at or bad at when it comes to coding and development in general.

One way to think about this is in Jurassic Park, when they have, they have gaps in the dinosaur DNA and it fills it in with frog DNA, kind of. LLMs do that too, where if you don't bring the specific examples, it just assumes based on the most generic background information of how it fits in. So they do really well. If you're writing it in the same kind of React style that everybody else writes it, they're really good at that because they've got tons of frog DNA to bring into your particular situation to get really good results. If you're writing an algorithm that's been represented is like nicely, cleanly separated and exists in lots of tutorials and Wikipedia articles or whatever. They're also pretty good at reproducing that. When you have an architecture, like a relatively large architecture that is slightly atypical or it has some weird stylistic and calling ways it's been designed, architected, they start getting a little bit funky there and a little bit like they can lose the plot of it. Remember that like LLMs can't count. And it's a miracle in some ways that they work reasonably, that they can handle some of these cases. One of the reasons like really deeply nested JSON, they lose track of, am I in an object context or an array context? And they'll like miss nest some of the things. Partially because it's just really hard to keep track of the counting. One way of looking at this is they do a really good job of seeing objects like that. So smaller chunks of JSON, they're shaped roughly like that, just in way more examples, because presumably the JSON you'd see on the web. It has a logarithmic kind of fall off of like the larger sizes get increasingly less, less common. And so it doesn't have as much training data to keep track of and it can't fundamentally count. And so it just kind of loses track of it. Just it's remember it's all like vibes based programming. It's just, it's vibes are extremely dialed in.

SPEAKER_2: Yeah. And it's fascinating because, like you mentioned, there's a lot of React code on the internet that has ended up in these models, and the like. And then I start to think about the frog DNA. It's, do we have the kind of 'me'frog? Because most of the DNA that's gotten in there with React code is from the average React developer, not like the React core developer that's out there. And so is there like that magical piece of like actually the shark DNA that hasn't evolved that much, right? It's like, it's just gotten really good and honed in. You really want something that's good enough, that it's got enough content that's out there, but is also really focused. I'm really curious about the notion of also just like king making as well. If we created a brand new language today, do we have a chance to make it? And how do you feel, how do you think about the role of data and quality data and golden path data, all of those things with LLMs?

I love the shark data. I'm just writing that down. The LLMs are really good at imitation. They're so insanely good at imitation that it sometimes looks like innovation, but they're really fundamentally kind of conceptually interpolating across just a very wide swath of human experience and examples. And it does feel like it pulls us towards the center. It pulls us towards the average, mushy center. Like when you're in its writing, its default writing style is like corporate speak because that's the mushy center that at the core, like the random, bland heat death average kind of thing, and we get the same thing in programming where we're just kind of, I don't know, I'm just going to use React. I'm going to just do it whatever the most basic way that you can possibly, like, basic in the pejorative sense. And I do think it, one thing that's interesting and challenging, like one of the ways that it knows how to weight what things are good is like things that have worked for others in the past are more likely to be replicated and other people to also talk about them and write about them. And so LLMs aren't random sampling all texts that could exist. They're random sampling the text that humans have found useful enough to propagate. Right. And you have this natural thing where humans are selecting to some degree, the thing that it's reading off of. And, but over time, as more and more that selection is done from like this just Bayesian kind of averaging process, you lose some of that signal. And it does feel like it will pull us more towards the centroid. It also does feel like to me, it will be much harder to escape local Maxima because it's just. In default, if I don't have a strong opinion about how to write this bit of code, and I said, do a thing that has this behavior, what's it going to reach for? It's going to reach for the most obvious basic answer of that and put it in there. I don't have a strong opinion, so I'm not going to go through. No, no, no, no. Do it in this other file that I think is interesting. And so that's fine. And so now you'll have yet another piece that is now put onto the whole collection of. Yeah. That's funny.

SPEAKER_2: Now I'm thinking about how to nudge selection to be like, this software passes tests a lot versus what are the other parts of selection that could help nudge things fast. So I've noticed in the past, you've mentioned that spending time to define requirements saves extraordinary amounts of time. And so I'm just curious, what does.

requirement just like when we talk about specs it means lots of different things to different people like what do you mean by that i think it's partially that was my direct experience in the last few weeks i realized it's a pattern i've seen many times like oh we understand how to build this i've got the idea in my head we will simply execute this it turns out exploring the requirements like you have like this hyper object In your head of this should work and all the pieces of it like this and explain it to other people is like serialization process. It takes so long and it's like a combinatorial kind of thing to really serialize it perfectly. And the way to communicate it is then factor that serialization into higher level requirements, which is a process that takes a ton of time. And so you're always like, oh, we're fine. We're fine. We're just going to, we're just going to wing it. I'm just going to give them enough guidance or whatever. But if you do this properly, you're like, 'oh, we will fast by not doing the requirements or factoring out with the worker requirements.'And what you end up with is you realize later that people didn't understand the fundamentals of the thing you're trying to do. And they went in these other weird quarters and you have to be like, 'No, actually there's a whole thing.'You can't do it that way. You gotta do it this way. And they go, 'okay, cool.'I had to throw out this entire thing I just did now. And so to them, it feels like a total thrash. messages.content: So much of the way that we tackle problems and build products is this builder mindset. It's like, I have a plan, I then manipulate things to match my plan and make it happen, and this is a way you can create tons of value. Part of the problem though is it can't possibly create more value than the effort that you put into it. What I look for instead are things that can be gardened, things that can grow on their own and that you can sort of direct or maybe give a little bit of extra energy to or curate over, and it's a totally different mindset for it. If you do this properly, it looks like magic. I've been told that this is completely against all the advice that people get building products nowadays. But I think it's a very powerful approach that works in a lot of different contexts.

Alex spent 13 years at Google where he worked on Search, DoubleClick; he led Chrome's open web platform team for eight years, led augmented reality within Google Maps, and developed a new toolkit to align company-wide strategy from the bottom up. After a stint at Stripe as head of corporate strategy, he's currently founding a startup that aims to reimagine the web for the AI era. In our wide-ranging conversation, we cover how LLMs and Gen AI will impact how we build product in the coming years, what skills will matter most as AI becomes a bigger part of our lives, what companies can learn from slime mold, organizational kayfabe, the adjacent possible, strategy salons, why you should be thinking more like a gardener than a builder, plus a bunch of productivity tips, life advice, and so much more. This was such a fun episode, and I am sure this is going to get your mind thinking in completely new ways. If you enjoy this podcast, don't forget to subscribe and follow it in your favorite podcasting app for YouTube. Thanks for having me.

I think they're truly a disruptive technology. It never changed that it's just a blank in your head. And now gravity has changed effectively for your perspective. And all kinds of intuition is now raw. I put this thing on the table, it's going to stay there. And then it slides off and falls into the wall. All kinds of weird stuff will happen. And I think LLMs to me feel like that. It changes the basis of competition. I think today I see a lot of folks using LLMs and LLMs are like a squishy computer. Now LLMs can do all kinds of stuff and they don't do exactly what you told them, but they do typically do kind of roughly what you meant. And the adjacent possible is a set of actions that you can do that are right in front of you that if you do them, they would work, almost certainly work. And in the tech industry in particular, we kind of default assume that the adjacent possible is like this, and you go, and like flying leap to something. Like, you know, you're saying don't do big things. But when you recognize that your adjacent possible is relatively small, you realize that you actually have full agency to pick within the subset that is within your reach. And your actions matter because you take an action and now the world reconfigures, and now you get a next set of actions. And it's based partially on the action you just took. And so if you slice this thing up and you have a coherent worldview and you have a principled approach, you can arc to wildly different outcomes than looked like they were possible. While at each point, each individual action is safe and reasonable. And so you can combine both of these things. I think so many times we try to like jump and we jump to the end state of the thing. And actually, you don't need to make that decision. Like if you can slice up your decisions into smaller and smaller decisions, and like this next step definitely makes sense. And if those other things don't happen, okay, don't take another step on that path. That's fine. Go in other directions for a while. messages.content: So I wanted to walk you through sort of how we think about technology and how it can help in these spaces. So I think historically, I want to start with the history of the web. So the web grew up on desktop. You had beefy machines. You were plugged into the power, so you didn't have to worry about battery life. You had a fast, always-on connection. And in that space, the web was really revolutionary because it helped users get access to businesses in a way that they couldn't do before with much, much, much lower friction. You didn't have to drive to the store. You didn't even have to install any software. You just opened up your browser, you clicked the link, and you were there. And that low friction is pretty key. I think we forget about it today, but it's something that's still incredibly critical to a lot of businesses online. So then, flash forward to the first iPhone, and at the beginning, everyone thought that the web was going to do the same thing on mobile. It's going to be the same basic thing, just on a smaller device that had battery, more of a limited CPU, and that kind of thing. In fact, the first iPhone, the way to build apps for it was actually web apps. But that didn't last for too long. And as everybody knows now, mobile is all about apps, right? It's all about having an app for that for everything. Now, I'm biased. Obviously, I work on browsers and have for eight or so years now. But I think that's not the entire picture. There's a lot more that's going on in this space. So forgive the abstract diagram here, but this is the way I think about this problem. So on the y-axis, you have value. This is more of like the abstract value that users get from interacting with businesses that they want to interact with and buy things from, and that businesses get from users who interact with them. And on the x-axis, you've got users, organized by how engaged they want to be with your business. So at the very far left, is people who clicked the link to your site accidentally, said, 'oops', bounced, and never came back. And on the very far right is the president of your fan club. And ideally, you want people to move up this curve over time. As they interact with your business, they want to become more and more engaged to a higher level. And it's also a relatively smooth curve. But if you look at this with native apps, I think this is the area that it actually covers. Your most engaged users are the ones who come back, they engage a whole lot in these really rich native app experiences. But of course, this is only one portion of the larger space. There's all this other gray area, users who want to engage with you but aren't able to in that space. Because with apps, you have this do or die moment. Is the user going to install my app, or are they not? And there's a lot of people that don't necessarily want to install your app. It's a big commitment. It takes up space on your phone. It takes up a slot on your home screen. And it turns out there are lots of users who just don't want to do it. So if you don't have a use case where users want to come to your site weekly or daily, they might just not want to come to your site. So we've seen a bunch of funnels like this. We talk about this a lot in the technology space. We know that some people will come to our apps and that they're going to drop out at some point in the funnel. So they'll come to our site. They'll tap a banner to go to the Play Store or the App Store. They'll decide to install it. They'll wait for it to install. At every point, you're losing people in this funnel. And if you look, it's very context-dependent. But if you look at different stages in this funnel, you generally see that the percent that actually make it through that stage is low single digits.and maybe at the highest 20%.So for example, Google Plus released a case study a few years ago and showed that if users came to the site, they saw a full screen interstitial that said, hey, go get the app. And only 9% of users clicked through that. And that's actually pretty high in the industry. And the other thing that's actually very misleading about this sort of classic chart is these steps compound. If you fall out of the first stage of the funnel, you don't make it to the second stage. Those low numbers actually multiply like crazy. This actually is much more realistic in terms of the number of users who are getting through your funnel. If you look at this original graph, it’s actually more like this in terms of the value that you’re creating, which isn’t that big in the grand scheme of things, obviously. If you’re Facebook, maybe this part of the graph is pretty big, but if you aren’t Facebook, it’s probably somewhat small. Also, for many businesses, if users don’t interact with you all that often, you might not have any space that people really want to install that app in general. So if you sell couches, that’s your primary thing. People don’t buy a couch every week. And so that’s not necessarily what they’re going to want to come back to that experience. There's tons of missing value here. But luckily, the web never went away on mobile. And so it's possible to build experiences that reach many users with that low friction. But historically, they haven't been great. Users come to them. They don't engage all that much. And part of the reason is because I think a lot of businesses take their desktop web app and they just shrink it down to the mobile space. And they say, yeah, we got a web app. People come to it. They don't really use it that much, I guess, on mobile. That's just how it works, I guess. And they kind of give up. But this picture does not feel right to me. There's so much value that's being left on the table for users and for businesses. It can't just be that you have a small number of highly engaged users and a whole bunch of people in this wasteland. When I think about what makes apps special, I think one thing that's really important that people don't think too much about is that when you invest in an app, you don't just shrink down your desktop experience. You really have to think from the ground up, what does the user actually want to do in this context, in this moment? Because on desktop, it's way more goal-directed. I want to buy a couch. I'm going to go research, open up a bunch of tabs, figure out what I want to do, then maybe search for prices, and then ultimately purchase. But on mobile, it's very different. People are generally on their devices in sort of this found time, time that I don't know what people did decades ago, to be quite honest, because it seemed like it would be really boring. But even in the home, you have people who are watching TV and looking at their mobile devices. At Starbucks, you place an order for a coffee. You've got maybe three minutes or so. You pull out your phone, and you're like, 'Hmm, Pinterest.'Or in the taxi, when you're just trying to kill time and try not to focus on how scary it is driving through city traffic sometimes. So in these contexts, if you're trying to sell couches, the way to do it is not to try to get the conversion right there at that moment when someone's just waiting for their coffee. That's probably not going to happen. It's more about helping people be inspired by the types of things they want to buy. So be able to immerse and say, 'Oh, that's really interesting. I hadn't really thought about that particular style or how these different types of furniture could go along together.'And I think that this is really important. It's a critical thing. It's the reason about your user in the context and in the moment that they're actually reaching you in the way they want to engage with you. And luckily, this is not app-specific. You can do this on your mobile websites as well. So I think a number of folks, as they've invested in this, they've seen that just by being really thoughtful about the way that they approach this value, they can have a much more immersive, much, much, much more engaging experience. It doesn't have to be this wasteland. But there's still this missing value. There's still this gray portion that web just can't do. And so a few years ago, a number of browser vendors and us, we got together and we tried to solve this problem and figure out what that missing piece was. And there's three major pieces. The first is connectivity. So on desktop, you always had a connection. You didn't have to worry about the data dropping. But on mobile, you are constantly on the go in different conditions. So people are like, 'Oh, no, I have LTE.'All my customers are premium customers. They've got LTE. But that doesn't mean that much because radio waves are influenced by metal and buildings and all kinds of stuff. So as you're walking down the street, you're coming in and out of connectivity. And if you're in an app, you generally have all the resources locally. So, when you tap, it just kind of loads a little bit more slowly. But on web, when you tap and you don't have connectivity, you see an error page, effectively. You see this offline dinosaur. You feel like you fell off the edge of the internet. And that's not a great experience. The next one is push notifications. Push notifications, when they came out, were kind of this interesting novelty that was great for chat applications. But it turns out that having timely information on the go that you can react to is perfect for people in those contexts. And so push notifications really are just a part of the fabric of what users expect in a mobile experience. And the last one seems kind of silly, but people really don't like typing on mobile. It's just more of a pain. So I'm sitting there at Starbucks, and I've got three minutes. Even if I would really love to go to Washington Post right now, I'm not going to do it. I'm going to open up my phone and go, hmm, Pinterest. Even though, had Washington Post been there as an option to fall back into, I might have chosen that, which would have been great for Washington Post and great for me. So we talked about a lot of these. In this space, standards take a long time. So it's been, I think, four or five years that we've been working with Mozilla and Microsoft and Samsung and Opera and many others. And the good news is that we've actually brought solutions in terms of new web standards for all of these. So I'm not going to go into the details about what exactly these are. Words like service workers or add to home screen or web push notifications. The point is, though, that these are optional. So you can build the best experience you want for your users in the context they are. And if they come to you in a browser that supports these new web standards, you can just make that experience much better. So that's, of course, surprise, the missing piece, that missing value that historically has not been possible to get. One thing that's really important about this is you notice there's a few jumps that are kind of discontinuities. And that represents the notion of, say someone's buying that couch. They've decided after a long time they've been inspired on multiple, in desktop, in person, maybe they've seen it in your retail store. And they're like, I want to purchase right now. And it's out of stock. So they don't want to install your app. It's way too much commitment. But if it says, 'Hey, do you want to get notified when this couch comes back in stock?'Sure. That sounds right. So that's an opportunity to create more engagement with that user in a way that's good for everybody. So these are jumps that are very difficult to get if you have to convince them to go all the way to app. So you can have a much smoother gradation and build that relationship with users over time. So I think this is a bit of a paradigm shift in terms of what we can do now. On desktop, historically, about a decade ago, there was another paradigm shift. This is the word that Luke said you wouldn't necessarily know, but we called it Ajax. It was this notion of building the best possible web experience you could, just that users would understand and expect a much more rich experience. And I think the same thing is happening on mobile. And we call it progressive web apps. So it's really about redefining what modern mobile web development means. It means holding yourself to a much higher standard, thinking through very carefully. The user experience, what users want to actually engage with you in the context, in the moment, in the place that they come to interact with you. And then, of course, in places that have these new web standards like Chrome, Firefox, Soon, Edge, and other browsers, giving them an even better experience than that. So we've seen across the world in the past year or so, basically in every vertical, we have a great example of a progressive web app in every market. Around the world, we've seen examples of these. In some markets, like India, basically every vertical has one of their major players has built a progressive web app. In the United States, folks like Forbes, Washington Post, Lyft, and most recently Twitter have shipped progressive web apps, and many more of them are on the way. And just a couple weeks ago, Gartner released a report talking about how important they were. And when you look at how these actually impact users, across the board, in every one that we've looked at, the numbers are pretty big. So this is Alibaba, which is an e-commerce player. And this is just one of the examples. We've got about 13 or 14 case studies published. And we see lots of companies are publishing their own case studies. They're just so excited by the numbers that they're seeing. And we're seeing, again, this huge engagement, this lift in conversions, in engagement, in time spent, just capturing more of that value for users. And it makes sense, right? Just by focusing on building the best experience you can, there's a lot more that you can do as opposed to just shrinking your desktop site down. So the reason I'm up here with Luke is because West Elm is a great example of a retailer who's on the forefront of thinking through this. And so they recently are rethinking how their mobile experience works and are building an awesome progressive web app. So he's going to tell you more about it.

In fact, when you see animations in here, it's actually using CSS animations. But you don't have to believe me, because I'm going to show you and prove to you it's true. What's new is putting it in the hands of web developers so they can do the same kind of stuff. So let's walk through the concepts and make it all work. The first thing here on this slide, I've got logical DOM that you're used to walking through with first child and get children and that kind of thing. And over here off the right in the purple is the Shadow DOM. So each element Each element in the DOM can now have a shadow element, a shadow root, hanging off to the side. But in reality, logically, if you walked through it, the green element would still be there. So that's not really that useful, right? Because I actually probably had content in there I actually wanted to show. And that's why Shadow DOM has one extra idea. Which is the notion of insertion points. So we define, and I'll tell you how in a second, which types of these elements we want to teleport into these holes when we render. So what effectively happens is when we render, you see a document, a composed document that looks like this. And then at the document load, you call this magical tab strip method. And that goes through and it hoists all those things. Only when we render it to the screen is when we logically sort of conceptually move it around. And you can see we have a select statement here. And the select statement actually just takes any CSS selector. Then we've got another content that says, anything that you didn't grab for that first selector, just print the rest of it in here. Select everything else and put it in here. Well, you would define it basically in a special document with this element tag, where you'd say I want my tag to be called xchoice, I want it to be basically like a div, And here's the JavaScript constructor for that object that I want you to call whenever you see it. Then you have a template element that just defines that Shadow DOM stuff right there. Then in your script, you can define methods on that object that you define. So this is how you as an author of components would define what a component is and how it acts. The end user, it's much more simple. They just say, okay.include a link to that document that the author wrote, and then inside their HTML, just use an x-pole, just like it's any other element. And then if they grab that from the DOM, if they say document.querySelector and get me all the x-poles, they get back an object that has all these methods on it. So instead of having this world where you have a JavaScript object and a DOM object, use a DOM object that has all the methods on it that you need. So one of the other parts that's hard about styling is that you need to be able to not have your styles leak out into the rest of the document and vice versa. And we have a tool for that as well, which is implicitly scoped styles. So a style that's inside of a template, inside of the shadow DOM, will by default only affect things inside of that shadow DOM. So here we have a style sheet with a selector that selects everything. It says, hey, make everything red. But it's only actually affecting this one paragraph. Because that's the only thing inside of here. The things outside of here are not affected. So that sort of makes sure that your styles that you define in your component don't accidentally go through and mess everything else up. But also, by default, styles that hit your component won't come through. There's a shield. It shields the document styles from you. So you don't have to worry about someone going through and changing all kinds of weird settings and stuff and reaching inside of your component and changing crap you don't want to change because you have this shield that protects it. So in my example I had, I was using an accordion for the control to switch between the voting page and the results page, but that's not exactly a good UI metaphor. All we're going to do is we're going to change and include the definition of the tab strip that we want to use, and then we're just going to go through and replace x-accordion in our code with x-tabs. So I can switch to Sublime, which is the one I use. I'm going to change this from x .accordion to x .tabs. I've already done the include correctly. And I'll refresh. There you go. And it switched into the tab strip automatically. So I did very little work as a component user to switch to a totally different type of component. There wasn't any kind of wrangling to get these JavaScripts to include correctly. They're just basically different tags I can use. Okay, so this stuff is all about making a component on your own page where you trust everything around it. But say that you want to include components on other pages. So one of the guys who works on this, who's actually the author of the Web Components spec, is this guy named Dimitri. And he has a very interesting design sensibility. Here's his blog. I don't know if I necessarily agree with all the style choices he's made, but he's developed his own design aesthetic. And he asked me before I came here, he was like, hey, I really want to be able to put one of your polls onto my page. I was like, cool, I want you to use the poll I wrote, but I don't necessarily want him to be able to go through and tweak all the styling and change around answers and stuff. So I don't want him to be able to peek inside of my component and change things. That's okay. What we're going to do, is we're going to change what's exposed in the DOM. So by default, everything in the shadow is just sort of hanging off to the side, anyone who wants can go poke around and change it. But it's actually possible to sort of hide that stuff, to put it in the shadow and then throw out the shadow, the reference to it, so no one else can get inside and mock it. So what I'm going to do is take my exact same component, I'm going to define a new component called AwesomePole for Dimitri's site, because I think he loves the word awesome and so he'll love this. And I'm going to basically just take it and take all that stuff I had to find in that last example and stuff it into the shadow of a new component. So when you look at it in the DOM, you aren't going to see anything. It's just X awesome poll and that's it. That's all that you see in the site. So Dimitri can't go through and change, for example, the question titles or the coloring or anything. So we'll go ahead and switch to his blog again. And here you go. You've got basically, you've got that exact same thing in his site embedded in there. But really, right now, all I've got basically is an iframe. You can do exactly this with an iframe, right? You already have guaranteed security boundaries. Why would you want to do it this way? Well, I can allow more customizability to Dimitri. So he said he wanted to change some of the text and make it fit in with the copy he likes to write. But I'm not really comfortable with him changing, say, the title of the question or some of the other stuff in here. Because I don't want him to be able to change the poll so it says, Vote for the semantics option because that's not what I want him to be able to do. But that's okay because Web Components allows me to decide which things I'm comfortable with him changing. So what I can do is define it so that when you include an aside, I'll take that and I'll use that as my subtitle for the question. And when you include a label, I'll use that and I'll make it the text for the button to submit the vote. But I decide, based on which insertion points I have in my Shadow DOM, which things I want to allow him to change. So I'm defining my own declarative API. If he were to go through and pop in an H1 here or something, let's ignore it and drop it on the floor. And so we'll do that. We'll switch to the example here. We'll uncomment it in this section. Save. And refresh. And now you'll see actually that he's changed it and said vote for CSS3 because it's awesome. And the vote now button has switched to his text that he wanted there. And again, I gave him control over small pieces of this component, but not all of them. But then he got angry at me when I showed him this because he said, 'Yeah, but the color scheme is kind of stupid.'He's got a point. It doesn't really fit in with all that well. But I don't know if I want him to go through and change around and mess with all the very carefully calibrated layouts I've gotten here. So I'm going to allow him to control parts of the layout, but only some parts. And the way I'll do this is with something called CSS variables. And you might have heard of CSS variables. You may have used them if you use Sass or Less or something like that. CSS variables is a new spec. It's actually going to start shipping in Chrome very soon, that actually allows you to basically do this in the browser directly without a preprocessor. Here's a very simple example: you define on the root, a variable called 'myColor'.You give it a color of green. You define 'myBorder'where it is one big solid with 'myColor', which would be green. And then elsewhere, you just use that variable right here. CSS variables is kind of misunderstood. It's actually incredibly powerful. It does all kinds of interesting things that you can do with having these values cascade differently and using them elsewhere. But this is all you need to know to understand how this example is going to work. I really do encourage you to go learn about CSS variables and figure out how they work. So now that we have that, what we can do as a component author is we can use variables inside of our components and define where in our style sheets we want to use these components. And then if a component user then targets a style at that component from the outside, we basically say, okay, those things that I've defined a variable for, I'm going to allow those styles to snake through that barrier I've created. So most styles that Dimitri tries to style at my component just sort of bounce right off. You can't get inside. But I've poked specific holes for him where I've said, I'm okay if you reach in here and tweak this thing. So in this case, I've said, I'm okay if you change the font or if you change one of the highlight color. So let's switch to his example and change that. Save and switch back. And now you can see that he's used it to make his heading a different color and the font is actually subtly Comic Sans, which in his words is the best font ever. So, what I've done here is I've defined a component that I've felt good about letting him use on his site. I give him very specific hooks to change just the things I'm comfortable with. I don't have to give him the keys to the kingdom and change everything around. I can define these very interesting declarative APIs. So, I've been talking about the tactical bits of Web Components. But I'm going to take a step back and tell you why I'm so excited about this stuff. And I call it the Declarative Renaissance. And sometimes people think I'm being a little bit melodramatic about this. But I really do feel strongly. That this Web Components as a technology will enable a very different kind of web ecosystem. So a lot of different ways. One of them is having these guardrails, not etiquette. So I was able to give Dimitri the guardrails and say, 'Here's the things I'm going to allow you to touch in my component,'instead of saying, 'Here's my component, and in the documentation say, pretty, pretty, pretty please don't touch these things or your thing might break.'I can direct him into where he's allowed to switch things. This is a subtle point, but if everyone is building on the same foundations, these same primitives, that means that widgets from different libraries will be compatible. So today they're all written in incompatible ways because there's no good way to express it, so they've all invented their own way of doing it. But here, if they're all using the DOM, if they're all using web components, this toolkit framework kind of, then they can build widgets that all just work together. So instead of having everyone split up and create eight different calendar widgets for the eight different UI toolkits and eight different tab strips, well, now you can mix and match as a user the ones that you like. So that means that people can focus on improving the ones that everyone already uses as opposed to building yet another tab strip. Multiple authors on the same page. So again, this is the guardrails, not etiquette. This means that you can write pages where you're comfortable, that even if other people come and change things up here, it's not going to affect inside of my thing. At Google, you should see the guidelines they have where it's like, if you do a style sheet, thou shalt do this, just so you don't accidentally mess up the toolbar along the top of all the properties. It's very complicated. But this allows it to be much easier. Making many uses fully declarative. So a lot of people don't have a CS degree. They know how to write HTML. They don't know how to write code. This allows them to very simply use these components and actually sometimes have them do very interesting things. Without writing a single bit of code, it's really powerful, declarative. Also goes faster, it's less code to ship down the wire because you're using more of the web platform to its full potential. It also means that we can write it in native code and just make it go faster. Script has gotten really fast, but it's never going to be quite as fast as native code. So, and then as we make optimizations, your thing gets faster automatically. Finally, the rise in shared semantics: so today, It's hard for people to say, 'Hey, I think we should have a tab strip,'and that should be part of the platform. That should be something that just ships in every browser. But if everyone can agree that this whole community can basically define these components that are useful to them, and everyone sort of stabilizes on the same basic semantics for how these things should work, well, that's a natural thing to promote and make into the HTML5 spec and become a part of the web platform. So this is the way for you guys to start experimenting and showing us which things you'd like to be in the platform and just ship inside the box with your browser. Shadow DOM and CSS variables are shipping in Chrome soon, CSS variables very soon. And the thing is that once you have these technologies, we can actually polyfill them pretty closely. The other thing too is that other browser vendors, including folks at Microsoft, folks at Mozilla, folks at other browsers, and all the standards bodies have been actively participating in the spec for the past couple of years. We've seen a great engagement from everyone. This is not something that Google is going off and doing on the side at all. This is something that's definitely on the road to becoming a specification that is well on the road to becoming a true standard. So, what you can do is help the future arrive faster. There's only one link on here if you take the short link. That's our Google Plus page for Web Components. It's where we post interesting articles about this kind of stuff, updates to the spec. Following the actual spec discussions is very, very difficult. It's very abstract and technical. But here's where we have interesting summaries to follow. And then we also have, Dimitri has written a really great and very readable intro to all the different technologies in Web Components. This is a very little spec. We'll tell you how to use that kind of stuff. You can read the discussions on the standardization lists if you really want to. It's kind of difficult. It's kind of hard to follow along. But the coolest thing is experimenting. So we have a polyfill right now that in browsers with Shadow DOM support, so in Chrome with an option turned on, basically all this stuff just works. In fact, that's how our demo works here, is we had Shadow DOM turned on and we just used this polyfill. So you can start experimenting with these concepts, providing feedback, saying, oh, this is really cool, or this is great, except for this one thing that's kind of weird. We can fix that in the spec if it's early enough. Then also Mozilla. A guy named Daniel Buchner at Mozilla has built a really cool library called Xtags. It basically polyfills just the custom elements section of web components. But it's actually a production thing that works in almost all browsers that you can use today. Dimitri Glasgow in particular. The guy with the blog. Absolutely. messages.content: I kind of joke that LLMs, which are magical and amazing, are kind of like having a trained circus bear in your kitchen that makes you porridge. It's a miracle that it works at all. But also, it's a wild animal that is in your kitchen and could ransack your kitchen or kill you. The porridge isn't even that good. What are you talking about? So how do you use LLMs not as something with agency, but something that has a magical duct tape?

SPEAKER_1: Dude, I'm extremely excited to have finally a recorded conversation with you because I learned something every time I interact with you. Twitter, your blog is amazing. Our first conversation sent off fireworks in my brain. So, I'm very excited to get another crack at this. And I think I have a lot to learn from you because I admire a lot of the domains that you work in and how far you've taken them. So, I'm really excited about this.
Cool. I'm excited to be here. Thanks for having me.

SPEAKER_1: You're very...like high-level introduction of yourself, I think on your Twitter bio or whatever is 'Gardener of Systems.'And I thought maybe you could introduce yourself by breaking that down a little bit.

Yeah, sure. I find one of the core things that is a commonality across a lot of the work I've done in my kind of academic career as an undergrad and then in industry, it comes down to. Systems thinking, gardening systems, swarms. I just gave a lecture a couple of weeks ago at Oxford on AI and philosophy, and the frame was swarms and under what conditions they show up and when they can create value and the tension with coherence. And I think that to really wrestle with systems, you have to, Danilo Meadows has this great quote of like, to engage with a system, you have to let go and just dance with the system. And I think that a lot of the approaches that we take in normal business are very instrumentalist. They're very first order. They require like kind of just heroic execution. And a lot of the ways to create magical outcomes are understanding systems and dancing with them. And one of the reasons I like the word gardening is it underlines that you are not in control of this system. You are influencing it. You have a meaningful impact on it, but it is a thing that is alive without you. And so often I think that we use the builder mindset when we're approaching products or problems. And that's why I like, the funny thing, of course, is my husband will tell you, my husband is a very good gardener and our garden is beautiful because of him. I am a terrible gardener of physical symptoms. And it's like, oh, gross, but you feel dirty and sweaty. And like, it takes forever to see any results. So I like the gardening metaphor more than I like the garden, gardening in an actual, practical reality.
SPEAKER_1: What is it you like about the metaphor? Is it the patience? Is it the inducement rather than the action?

Yeah, it's reacting to, I think people often have, I wrote a very short essay because most of my stuff I write is very long and discursive. I wrote a very short one a few months ago called The Magic of Acorns. And the overall frame of it is a parable about the builder and the gardener. And the two of them are both trying to build large, beautiful things. And the builder gets immediately to work and says, 'Okay, here's some rocks I can pick up.'And here's the heaviest one I can carry and pulls it together. And, you know, with sweat on his brow, he's getting stronger and more clever about how to use leverage to move rocks in place. And the gardener's just sitting there, you know, dilly-dallying and looking at stuff at his feet and looking at picking up little pebbles. It's like, what are you doing, man? And the builder keeps on making more and more progress, getting stronger and more clever. And at a certain point, though, the gardener starts creating outcomes that start becoming large and beautiful. And the builder thinks, like, how the? Are they cheating? Like, are they doing it when I'm not watching? What's going on? And this thing gets bigger and bigger and more and more beautiful. And the secret, of course, is the gardener understands that other things can be alive, and that some of those pebbles are actually acorns. And that's totally unlike a pebble and an acorn are totally unlike each other. And then the acorn has the potential to be alive and to be this big, massive thing that grows on its own. It takes a little bit of tending and care and finding the acorns and watering them and putting them in good soil. But that looks very unlike the processing.building that entire tree yourself. And it's a very different mindset. And it's highly atypical for us to use in serious business. Serious business is all about heroics and sprinting around and having frameworks and saying, I definitely know the answer. In the gardening mindset, it's much more about acknowledging I don't know the answer. And yet I still can assert that I can make good outcomes happen. Like a garden requires a gardener. A gardener is somebody who pulls weeds, trims back things, plants certain seeds in places that things might be on without without saying that it builds that thing. It takes time. It takes reacting to this living system. But you can do amazing things with it if you just recognize that you are not building that system, that there's an alive thing outside of you that you're interacting with.
SPEAKER_1: Do you think there are are there leaders or CEOs or founders that you would classify as archetypal gardeners today? Or do you think we're still far from that ideal?

I think I wrote an essay last year that frames the Saramons and the Radagasts as two types of real magic. And most founders, most CEOs are canonically the former type, the Saramons, the builders, than the gardeners. And I think there's a structural reason for that. Like just to rehash the overall argument, it's, you know, we talk all the time about magic. Magic obviously isn't real in the physical world, like duh. But in the social world, it absolutely is real. And the social world is where we make most of the decisions that then lead into the physical world. So in a very real sense, magic affects the physical world. And in this essay, I described two different archetypes of real magic that work for, both of them work for game theoretic reasons. They work for totally different reasons. And the first is the Saruman. And this is the typical Steve Jobs, canonical Saruman. It's the heroic, powerful, insightful startup leader. And somebody who believes in the great man theory of history, one. Two, believes that they themselves are a great man. And three, has enough initial success that leads others to believe that they might be a great man. And then this can become a self-accelerating thing where enough people believe it's true that they take actions, assuming that it will be true, which causes it to become true. It's like a reality distortion field that follows these leaders. And the way it works game theoretically is a complete and total absence of self-doubt. And of course, this is one of the ways that you get Steve Jobs-style, massive dent in the universe kind of outcomes that create all kinds of amazing value. The vast majority of people who attempt to use this magic are just jerks. They're just annoying jerks that people don't like working with. But the vast, vast, vast majority of them are that. The ones that really make a difference use this magic. The second type of magic is the Radagast. And I don't know Lord of the Rings that well, except for I've watched the movies a couple of times. I actually asked Claude to help me unpack the backstory. Turns out Saruman is, I'm talking about Saruman before the Lord of the Rings movies. Back when Saruman was just an industrious, powerful wizard and before he had his kind of like evil turn and descended into evilness, which Claude tells me has a rich history of many thousands of years that he was in that mode. But the Radagast in the movies is, you know, the guy who's in the, the wizard who's in the woods and it's like, it's even magic. And he's got like, you know, bird poop on him and he's high all the time or whatever. And the Radagast magic works for a totally different world. This is the gardener magic and it works by loving everything and everyone around you and seeing seeds of greatness everywhere you look and helping develop those seeds of greatness into something that's larger, you know, that grows has been larger than the sum of its parts. And this magic fundamentally to wield it, you have to let go of the idea of getting credit for what you do, so it's like it's fundamentally an indirect effect. It's possible to do this very easily. It's possible to do this with very low risk, actually. The problem is that when it happens, people will say, oh, you just got lucky. You just happened to be there when that miracle happened. And no, it's like I'm farming miracles. I'm structurally creating the potential for miracles on a continuous basis. I can tell you why it works. I can tell you why. But whenever it happens, it will always look like luck. Uh and if you're okay with that, if you're okay with the you know performance review system at your company not not recognizing that effort then you can create do real magic who do you who are great, I love the frame of miracle farmers uh who are the who are the best farmers of miracles that you've seen?
SPEAKER_1: Like, who are the Steve Jobs? Or is it the nature of them that we don't know who they are because they're very quiet?

They're often indirect and behind the scenes. And they're often, there are a number of good examples. And somebody just gave me a really good example the other day that was like, oh, duh, yeah, they should add to the essay, they forgot. But like, you know, Steve Wozniak is a great example, Stuart Brand, Kevin Kelly, these kinds of people who inspire and catalyze something much larger than themselves. But by its very nature, it has to be to some degree slightly behind the scenes kind of thing. Like the big brash, big personalities are almost inherently must be the Saruman types because it's so obvious. It's not like, oh, they just got lucky and were there. It's like, no, they moved the mountain. You watch them do the thing. Whereas the Radagast, if you aren't watching too carefully, you'll think that they were just along for the ride or just, you know, doing something totally unrelated. And it's, so it's, I think on a structural level, like one of the things in the essay is like, no Radagasts own their own helicopters. The only people who are wealthy enough to own their own helicopters are Sarumans. Which is, by the way, one thing I don't like about this metaphor is, again, it emphasizes good versus evil. No, no, no. Saruman, the industrious pre-corruption Saruman. This is a powerful force to create good in the world, too.
SPEAKER_1: The ones that came to mind for me, I don't know them deeply well enough to know if they fit your metaphor, but D-Hawk. Yes.

Okay. Yes. A hundred percent. D-Hawk. Perfect. Perfect example. You know, D-Hawk was one of the people who I didn't know about before I worked at a payments company. And the more I learned, the more I was like, oh my God, damn, I want to meet this guy. Unfortunately, I never met him before he passed, but I knew a bunch of people who knew him quite well. And a hundred percent chaotic organizations, like the whole, his whole vibe for is a hundred percent. I should just add that to the essay.

SPEAKER_1: Yeah, it was a very organic sort of emergent management style that he had. And he, I think, he had a grand slam with Visa and then just left and became, I think, a farmer, literally like an orchard, like just started like tending his trees at the peak of his success, actually, just like very quietly left and was just like, 'I'm good.
